    By Content Type

    Content TypeEmailsTotal Sent Open RateClick RateCTOR

    Announcement

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do 96 61.5% 2.1%
    Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility 245 48.6% 0.8%
    Coming soon: Standard payer names will be required for all enrollment records 244 48.1% 1.2%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding 146 46.6% 0.0%
    Manage terminations & billing with more clarity 94 45.7% 2.1%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset share two traits: they address urgent, compliance-driven actions (e.g., state licensing exits, required field deadlines) or carry strong personalization signals (advisory board invites, thank-you notes), and they are sent to smaller, targeted segments. The highest open rates cluster around emails where the recipient has a clear, immediate reason to act — regulatory changes, exclusive membership access, or direct feedback requests. Broader list sends consistently underperform, suggesting that audience segmentation is a more reliable lever than subject line optimization alone. Going forward, prioritize segmenting announcements by relevance tier and lead with urgency or exclusivity cues when they are genuinely present.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and compliance-oriented subject lines drive the highest open rates among mid-size sends.

    61.5% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC'

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with explicit urgency markers ('Action required,' 'Upcoming changes,' 'Coming soon') when the announcement has a real deadline or compliance implication — don't soften the language.

    Funding announcement emails significantly underperform regardless of subject line variation.

    Average open rate of 18.1% across four '$43M raised' variants sent to 45–1,077 recipients

    Try this next: Deprioritize standalone funding announcement emails to existing customers; if sending, embed the news within a product-value context rather than leading with the dollar figure.

    Larger audience sends (500+ recipients) consistently underperform smaller, segmented sends.

    Emails sent to 500+ recipients average 27.5% open rate vs. 44.8% for sends under 300

    Try this next: Segment announcement lists by product area, role, or compliance relevance before sending — avoid blasting the full list for announcements that only affect a subset of customers.

    'Now live' and feature-launch subject lines produce moderate open rates but near-zero click rates.

    Three 'Now live' or new-feature emails average 39.2% open rate but 0.0–0.0% click rate

    Try this next: For 'Now live' announcements, test a more specific benefit-oriented CTA in the email body and ensure there is a single, prominent link rather than a general informational format.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized or exclusive-access subject lines achieve the highest raw open and click rates in the dataset.

    76.5% open rate and 58.8% click rate for 'Thank You + Quick Feedback Request'
    Show reasoning

    The top two open rates belong to small-list emails (17 and 29 recipients) with personal or exclusive framing — a feedback request and a CAB member kit. These numbers are compelling but the tiny send sizes make them statistically unreliable as a benchmark for broader campaigns. The pattern is directionally consistent with the urgency theme but the small N is a meaningful caveat.

    Reminder emails to small, engaged segments maintain solid open rates without cannibalizing prior sends.

    41.4% open rate for 'Reminder: February 11 Medallion Advisory Board Session' (29 recipients)
    Show reasoning

    The reminder email to the CAB segment (29 recipients) achieved 41.4% open rate, only slightly below the original welcome email to the same group (58.6%). This suggests reminder sends to warm, small audiences retain engagement rather than causing fatigue, but the sample is too small to generalize broadly.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates found across both short ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request', 43 chars) and long ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do', 65 chars) subjects

    High and low performers span the full range of subject line lengths. The confounding effect of audience size and content urgency makes it impossible to isolate length as a driver of performance from this data alone.

    Send timing (day of week or time) cannot be evaluated from this dataset.

    No send time data available across any of the 31 emails

    The dataset includes only send dates, not times, and the dates are spread across multiple days of the week without enough volume per day to compare. No reliable timing pattern can be extracted.

    Data quality notes: Several important caveats apply: (1) Two emails show 0% open and click rates with send sizes of 1 and 6 — these are almost certainly test sends or delivery failures and should be excluded from benchmarking. (2) The '$43M raised' announcement was sent as four separate variants on the same date to different segments, inflating the count of 'announcement' emails and making it hard to isolate subject line effects from audience differences. (3) The top two open rates come from sends of 17 and 29 recipients — statistically unreliable for drawing broad conclusions. (4) Two pairs of emails share identical subject lines ('New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' and 'New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing') but were sent to different segment sizes on different dates, suggesting A/B or re-send testing; results differ substantially, likely due to audience composition rather than subject line differences. (5) Without demographic or role data per segment, it is impossible to fully disentangle audience quality effects from messaging effects.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 1,124 47.3% 2.2%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 998 44.4% 2.0%
    ☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue? 1,201 37.5% 2.4%
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 1,793 34.1% 2.5%
      Test your operational readiness with this worksheet 83 33.7% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this dataset share two clear traits: personalized or event-style framing (invitations, coffee breaks, final-chance urgency) and a direct financial or operational consequence tied to the subject line. Broad-list blasts to 3,000+ recipients consistently underperformed, while smaller, more targeted sends—even on the same topic—achieved dramatically higher open rates. The data suggests that segmentation and urgency-framing matter more than topic alone, since 'provider readiness' and 'time to revenue' themes appear at both the top and bottom of the list depending on how they are framed and to whom. Future campaigns should prioritize smaller, targeted segments with consequence-driven subject lines over large undifferentiated blasts.

    Strong Signal

    Invitation and urgency framing in subject lines is strongly associated with higher open rates.

    47.3% open rate for '[You're invited]' vs. 6.6% for a broad pun-based subject to a similar topic list

    Try this next: Frame future content emails as invitations or time-limited events with explicit urgency cues (e.g., '[You're invited]', '[Final chance]') rather than declarative or curiosity-gap subject lines.

    Pun-based or playful subject lines sent to large lists perform poorly.

    6.6% open rate for 'A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays' sent to 3,852 recipients

    Try this next: Avoid wordplay or pun-based subject lines in B2B sends; use direct, benefit-oriented or event-style language instead.

    Shorter, more targeted audience segments consistently outperform large broad-list sends on the same topics.

    Emails sent to under 1,300 recipients averaged ~28% open rate vs. ~14% for emails sent to 3,000+ recipients

    Try this next: Segment large lists into smaller, high-relevance cohorts before sending, especially for content emails where open rate is the primary KPI.

    Repeated subject lines sent to different segments show meaningful open rate variance, suggesting list quality is a key variable.

    Same '[Report]' subject sent three times on the same date: 34.1%, 29.5%, and 13.1% open rates across different segment sizes

    Try this next: Prioritize list hygiene and segment by engagement history before every send; track open rates by segment rather than by campaign aggregate to surface which audiences are most responsive.

    Moderate Signal

    Bracket-label prefixes like '[Report]' and '[You're invited]' correlate with above-average open rates.

    34.1% and 29.5% open rates for '[Report]' sends vs. 13.1% for a third '[Report]' send to a larger list
    Show reasoning

    Bracketed labels appear on four of the top seven emails by open rate, suggesting the format signals clear value. However, the same '[Report]' subject line sent to 1,103 recipients achieved only 13.1%, indicating that audience segment and list size confound the effect of the label alone.

    Financial quantification in subject lines (e.g., '$1M+') is associated with the single highest open rate in the dataset.

    47.3% open rate for the subject containing '$1M+'
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing email is the only one that includes a specific dollar figure, and it achieved the highest open rate. However, with only one data point using this technique, it is impossible to isolate the financial figure from the invitation framing or the smaller, targeted audience as causal factors.

    Content themes around financial impact and operational bottlenecks outperform general awareness themes.

    'State-by-state payer delays and what they're costing you' achieved 30.6% vs. 'Why care access is breaking down' at 16.5% to a much larger list
    Show reasoning

    Emails that connect directly to revenue loss, enrollment delays, or cost consequences tend to appear in the upper half of the open rate rankings. More abstract awareness-framing subjects ('Why care access is breaking down', 'The hidden crisis behind hospital closures') cluster in the lower half. However, audience size differences make direct causal attribution difficult.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear directional pattern in this dataset.

    Top subject line has 78 characters; bottom has 46 characters

    Subject line character counts vary across the full performance spectrum with no consistent pattern. Short subjects appear at both high and low open rates, as do long subjects. The data does not support a length-based recommendation for this audience.

    Send timing (day or month) does not produce a reliable pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rate (47.3%) on 2026-04-30; bottom open rate (6.6%) on 2026-05-14—both in the same month

    High and low performers are distributed across multiple months (July 2025 through June 2026) with no clear seasonal or day-of-week advantage visible. The dataset does not include send-time data, and confounding variables like audience segment and subject framing dominate the variance.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines were sent multiple times to different audience segments on the same date (notably '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' sent three times on 2026-01-21, and 'Test your operational readiness with this worksheet' sent three times on 2025-08-21), making it impossible to isolate subject line performance from segment quality. Audience sizes range from 65 to 3,852 recipients, creating a strong size-vs-engagement confound throughout the dataset—smaller sends may reflect warmer or more targeted lists rather than a subject line effect. With only 31 emails total and multiple repeated subjects, statistical confidence is limited; directional findings should be validated with controlled A/B tests on matched audience sizes before being adopted as firm rules. Some subject lines contain encoding artifacts (e.g., '\xa0') suggesting formatting issues in the original sends that may have affected rendering and open rates.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 79 44.3% 1.3%
    In Vegas? Join us for Medallion’s Emerald Evening: A Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience 70 24.3% 5.7%
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 304 22.1% 3.4%
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 1,463 12.8% 3.2%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 1,336 12.2% 0.4%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset share two traits: small, highly targeted send lists (under 310 recipients) and subject lines that name a specific, compelling venue or experience rather than generic 'exclusive dinner' framing. The top three emails by open rate were all sent to 304 or fewer recipients, suggesting audience precision matters more than volume. To improve future campaigns, prioritize tighter segmentation and lead with the specific event hook (venue name, unique experience) rather than formulaic invitation language. Casual, conversational subject lines like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' also outperformed longer, formal alternatives at comparable list sizes.

    Strong Signal

    Smaller send lists consistently produce higher open rates, with the top performers all under 310 recipients.

    Top 3 emails by open rate: 44.3% (n=79), 24.3% (n=70), 22.1% (n=304) vs. median open rate of ~7% for sends over 900

    Try this next: Cap micro-event invite lists at 300 or fewer recipients by tightening ICP filters before sending, rather than broadening reach to maximize coverage.

    Naming the specific venue or unique experience in the subject line correlates with higher open rates than generic 'exclusive dinner' language.

    'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' hit 44.3% and 22.1% open rates; 'A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit' hit 12.2% and 11.3%

    Try this next: Always lead with the specific venue name or unique experience hook in the subject line rather than defaulting to 'exclusive dinner' or audience-descriptor framing.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, conversational subject lines punch above their weight compared to formal invitation copy at similar list sizes.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (n=489) opened at 12.0% vs. 'Don't Miss: An Exclusive Evening with Nashville Healthcare Executives' (n=472) at 4.7%
    Show reasoning

    The informal 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' format outperformed several longer, formal subject lines at comparable list sizes. However, this pattern is based on a limited number of casual-format emails, and confounding factors like audience segment and timing cannot be ruled out.

    Urgency and scarcity subject lines ('Final Seats,' 'Last Call,' 'Last Chance') do not reliably rescue low-performing campaigns.

    'Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston' opened at 12.8% (n=1,463), but 'Last Chance: Exclusive Evening with Tennessee Healthcare Executives' opened at only 3.7% (n=946)
    Show reasoning

    Urgency framing produced mixed results: one high-volume send with 'Final Seats' achieved 12.8% — good for that list size — while multiple 'Last Call' and 'Last Chance' emails clustered at the bottom. The campaign topic and audience quality appear to be stronger determinants than urgency framing alone.

    Unique experiential events (sporting events, theatrical experiences) generate higher click rates than standard dinners.

    'Emerald Evening: A Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience' achieved 5.7% click rate and 2.0% click rate across two sends; most dinner-only emails are under 1.5%
    Show reasoning

    The two Sphere Experience emails produced the top two click rates in the dataset (5.7% and 2.0%). Golden Knights experience emails, however, showed 0.0% click rates on two sends, so this is not universal across all experiential formats. The Sphere event's novelty may be a differentiating factor.

    Follow-up emails in a sequence show diminishing open rates but can still convert if the list is fresh.

    Boston Healthcare sequence: first email 10.0% open rate, second 12.8%, final call 6.6% — declining by the final send to the largest list (n=1,238)
    Show reasoning

    Across several multi-email sequences (Boston Healthcare, Nashville, Atlanta, Golden Knights), later sends in the sequence generally trend toward lower open rates, though the Boston sequence saw a mid-sequence spike on a 'Final Seats' send. This pattern is consistent with list fatigue but is complicated by the fact that later sends sometimes went to larger or different lists.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines do not clearly outperform longer ones when controlling for other factors.

    Top open rate (44.3%): 'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' is 55 characters; bottom open rate (3.0%): 'Don't Miss: An Exclusive Evening with Top Healthcare Executives' is 61 characters

    Both short and long subject lines appear across the full range of open rates. The casual short format 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' performs well, but so does the longer Flour + Water subject line. The data does not isolate length as a reliable predictor of open rate independent of content and list size.

    Send timing and day-of-week patterns cannot be assessed from this data.

    Dates span July 2025 to May 2026 with no clear performance clustering by month or season

    The dataset provides send dates but no day-of-week or time-of-day metadata. Additionally, the mix of event types, list sizes, and audience segments across dates makes it impossible to isolate timing as a variable. No actionable timing pattern is visible in this data.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple sends with different list sizes (e.g., 'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' sent to both 79 and 304 recipients; 'A Seat at the Table: Aquavit' sent to 1,336 and 1,380; 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent to 85, 489, and 610), making it difficult to attribute performance to subject line wording versus audience targeting or sequencing order. List sizes vary enormously — from 23 to 1,466 — which heavily influences open rate and makes direct comparisons unreliable without controlling for audience size and quality. The dataset covers only 31 sends, which is a small sample for drawing statistically significant conclusions, and no information is available on audience segment definitions, send timing within day, or event geography match to recipient location.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Your December Roundup ✨ 235 41.9% 1.3%
    Your November Roundup ✨ 202 41.8% 2.5%
    Your July Roundup ✨ 236 40.2% 2.1%
    Your August Roundup ✨ 316 34.0% 0.9%
    Your October Roundup ✨ 305 32.9% 0.3%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing newsletter emails share a personalized, possessive framing ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨') and were sent to smaller, likely more targeted audiences, achieving open rates of 40–52% compared to 27–32% for the broader 'Medallion Product Update' sends. The data suggests that as the list scaled from ~200–300 recipients to 1,200–1,400, open rates declined materially, raising the question of whether list segmentation or personalization degraded at scale. The sparkle emoji subject line format consistently outperformed the plain product update format in open rates, though click rates are low across the board, signaling that driving post-open engagement remains a challenge regardless of format.

    Strong Signal

    Personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines outperform branded product update lines on open rate.

    40.2–52.2% open rate for Roundup emails vs. 27.6–32.0% for Medallion Product Update emails

    Try this next: Continue using 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' framing or a close equivalent for newsletter subject lines, especially when sending to segmented audiences.

    Shorter, informal subject lines ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨') outperform longer, brand-forward ones ('Medallion Product Update - [Month] [Year]').

    Average open rate 40.5% (Roundup, ~5 words) vs. 30.0% (Product Update, ~5 words including brand name)

    Click rates are consistently low across all emails regardless of open rate performance.

    Click rates range from 0.3% to 4.3%, with only one email exceeding 2.5%

    Try this next: Audit the in-email CTAs and content structure to determine why opens are not converting to clicks, particularly for the larger Product Update sends where click rates are 0.8–1.3%.

    The single-recipient July Roundup send (n=1, 0% open rate) appears to be a test or error send.

    1 recipient, 0.0% open rate, 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Exclude test sends from performance reporting to avoid skewing aggregate metrics.

    Moderate Signal

    Open rates decline as list size scales from hundreds to over 1,000 recipients.

    Open rates drop from 40%+ (n=23–316) to 27–32% (n=1,260–1,385) as audience scales 4–5x
    Show reasoning

    The four largest sends (1,260–1,385 recipients) all achieved lower open rates than nearly all smaller sends. This could reflect list quality dilution, less segmentation, or regression to a broader mean as a list grows. However, it also coincides with the subject line format change, so the two effects cannot be fully separated.

    No Signal

    There is no clear send-day or send-timing pattern that explains open rate variation in this dataset.

    Emails sent across Tuesday through Monday with no consistent day-of-week advantage visible

    The data does not include send time (hour), and send dates span different days of the week without enough repetition per day to isolate a timing effect. Open rate variation is better explained by audience size and subject line format than by send timing.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is limited to 12 sends, with significant audience-size skew: Roundup emails ranged from 23 to 316 recipients while Product Update emails ranged from 1,260 to 1,385, making direct open-rate comparisons between the two formats confounded by list composition and size. The June Roundup (n=23) is too small to be statistically reliable despite its 52.2% open rate. 'Your July Roundup ✨' appears twice — once as a legitimate send (n=236, 2025-08-06) and once as an apparent test send (n=1, 2025-08-08) — which should be excluded from aggregate analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing onboarding emails share a problem-framing approach in their subject lines — they name a specific friction point or inefficiency affecting providers rather than leading with features or savings. Curiosity-gap phrasing like 'silent slowdown' and 'smarter onboarding' outperformed explicit benefit claims like 'Save up to 64% on cost.' The highest click-to-open outlier ('The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about') suggests that insider, conversational framing may drive action beyond just opens. Next time, prioritize subject lines that name a hidden or underappreciated problem over those that quantify a benefit or describe infrastructure.

    Strong Signal

    Emails sent to very small audiences (3–5 recipients) produce unreliable performance signals.

    4 emails sent to 5 or fewer recipients, including two with 0.0% open rate

    Try this next: Only use performance data from sends of 15+ recipients when making creative decisions about subject line strategy.

    Moderate Signal

    Problem-framing subject lines outperform explicit benefit or feature claims on open rate.

    37.9% vs 0.0% open rate comparing top and bottom performers
    Show reasoning

    The two highest-opening subjects ('What smarter onboarding looks like' and 'The silent slowdown affecting your providers') frame a problem or gap, while the zero-open subjects led with explicit benefits ('Save up to 64%') or described infrastructure. The pattern is directional but the sample is small and audience sizes vary significantly across sends.

    Subject lines with a specific, named bottleneck or friction point drive the highest click rates.

    18.2% click rate for 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about'
    Show reasoning

    This subject line produced the only meaningfully high click rate in the dataset. Its 'no one talks about' phrasing signals insider knowledge, which may motivate clicks beyond mere curiosity. However, it was sent to only 11 recipients, so the absolute number of clicks is very small.

    Quantified savings claims in subject lines did not generate opens in this dataset.

    0.0% open rate for 'Credential once. Share securely. Save up to 64% on cost.'
    Show reasoning

    The one subject line explicitly quantifying a cost saving received zero opens despite being sent to 4 recipients. This is consistent with the broader pattern of benefit-forward subjects underperforming, though the tiny send size means this is not conclusive on its own.

    'Onboarding' and 'provider' language in subject lines correlates with higher opens, while 'credentialing' language correlates with lower opens.

    Average open rate ~27% for 'onboarding/provider' subjects vs ~10% for 'credentialing' subjects (excluding tiny sends)
    Show reasoning

    Subjects mentioning onboarding or provider dynamics (e.g., 'smarter onboarding,' 'silent slowdown affecting your providers') cluster at the top of the open-rate rankings. Most subjects using the word 'credentialing' fall in the lower half. This could reflect audience fatigue with credentialing terminology or a preference for outcome-focused framing. Caveat: the samples per word are small.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open or click rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~35 to ~65 characters with no consistent performance gradient

    High and low performers appear across short and longer subject lines. For example, 'A modern approach to revenue cycle performance' (46 chars, 27.3% open) and 'What smarter onboarding looks like' (34 chars, 37.9% open) both perform well, while longer lines also appear in the zero-open group. No length-based pattern is supportable here.

    Send timing (day) shows no distinguishable pattern given all emails were sent on the same two days.

    10 of 11 emails sent on 2025-12-09, 1 on 2025-12-10

    Nearly all emails were sent on the same date, making it impossible to assess send-day effects. The three emails sent on or around 2025-12-10 span the full range of performance outcomes, but that is too small a variation to draw conclusions from.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 11 emails with very small send sizes ranging from 3 to 37 recipients — far below the threshold needed for statistically reliable conclusions. Several sends had 5 or fewer recipients, meaning open and click rates for those emails are essentially noise. All emails were sent on the same day (2025-12-09) or one day later, eliminating any possibility of detecting send-timing effects. No subject lines appear to be exact duplicates, but some may have been sent to different segments of the same audience, which could introduce selection bias if certain segments are inherently more engaged.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    New profile completion logic is now live 78 55.1% 10.3%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing 94 51.1% 0.0%
    A better analytics experience is coming your way 180 48.3% 0.0%
    A new approach to profile completion will go live soon ✅ 102 44.1% 0.0%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing 74 33.8% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this set share two traits: they announce something already live (rather than previewing something coming), and they reference specific, named features rather than abstract improvements. The highest open rate (55.1%) went to a short, concrete subject announcing that profile completion logic was live, while the lowest open rate (33.8%) went to a repeated send of an analytics announcement — suggesting list fatigue from re-sending the same subject line is a real risk. To improve future sends, lead with 'is now live' language and avoid re-sending identical subject lines to overlapping audiences.

    Strong Signal

    Re-sending the same subject line to a different segment one week later produces a significantly lower open rate.

    33.8% vs. 51.1% open rate for identical subject lines sent one week apart

    Try this next: Vary the subject line when re-sending to a new or overlapping segment — at minimum, adjust wording to signal it is a reminder or clarification rather than a duplicate announcement.

    Moderate Signal

    Subjects announcing something already live outperform subjects previewing upcoming changes.

    55.1% vs 44.1% open rate for live vs. upcoming announcement on the same feature
    Show reasoning

    The profile completion 'now live' email (55.1% OR) outperformed the earlier 'will go live soon' teaser (44.1% OR) by 11 percentage points. This is directionally clear but rests on a single feature pair, so it cannot be stated with full confidence across all feature types.

    Specific, functional subject lines (naming the exact feature) outperform vague, benefit-framing subject lines.

    55.1% open rate for 'New profile completion logic is now live' vs. 48.3% for 'A better analytics experience is coming your way'
    Show reasoning

    The most concrete subject line — naming 'profile completion logic' directly — achieved the highest open rate, while the most abstract subject line ('a better analytics experience') achieved a mid-range rate. However, the emails went to different audience sizes, which may confound the comparison.

    Click rates are near-zero for all emails except the top-performing one, suggesting CTA or content engagement is broadly weak.

    4 of 5 emails have 0.0% click rate; only 'New profile completion logic is now live' achieved 10.3%
    Show reasoning

    Only the highest-open-rate email generated any clicks at all. This could mean the other emails lacked a clear or compelling CTA, or that the content did not give readers a reason to click through. However, it is also possible that product release emails in this category are primarily informational and clicks are not a primary goal for most sends.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines do not show a clear open-rate advantage over longer ones in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from 35 to 77 characters with no consistent correlation to open rate

    The top-performing subject ('New profile completion logic is now live', ~39 chars) is short, but the second-best performer ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing', ~76 chars) is among the longest. There is no monotonic or consistent relationship between length and open rate across these five emails.

    Send timing (day of week or time) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    Dates span July–September 2025 with no repeated day-of-week tested

    Each email was sent on a different date and there is no information about send time. With only five data points across different dates and no controlled day-of-week comparison, no timing conclusion can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: This analysis covers only 5 emails with audience sizes ranging from 74 to 180 recipients — all small lists where a few individual responses can swing rates significantly. Two emails share an identical subject line and were sent one week apart (Sept 10 and Sept 17), making it impossible to separate subject-line effects from audience-overlap or fatigue effects. The largest list (180 recipients on Aug 5) may represent a broader or less-targeted segment, which could depress its open rate independent of subject line quality. No send-time data is available, limiting any timing analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Share your experience with Medallion? 156 20.1% 1.9%
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 5,447 19.7% 3.0%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 4,888 19.1% 1.1%
    Your insights are needed! 6,006 17.6% 2.1%
    Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments 95 16.0% 2.1%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails in this dataset consistently achieve open rates of 17–20% when they combine a clear value proposition or direct ask with the Medallion brand name in the subject line. Personalization tokens and monetary incentives appear in high-performing emails but are not uniformly the differentiator — urgency framing ('Last call') and direct asks ('Your insights are needed!') perform similarly well. The outlier to avoid is 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20,' which sent to the largest audience (10,608) but achieved only 5% open rate, suggesting that vague, generic phrasing undercuts even a strong incentive. Future campaigns should prioritize specific, action-oriented language and avoid generic benefit-first subject lines.

    Strong Signal

    Specific or branded subject line wording outperforms generic benefit-first phrasing.

    5.0% open rate for 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' vs. 19.7% for '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!'

    Try this next: Always lead with a specific, direct ask or brand reference rather than a generic benefit statement; even when an incentive is present, frame it around the recipient's contribution ('your insights') rather than the reward alone.

    Moderate Signal

    Monetary incentive framing drives higher click rates but not reliably higher open rates on its own.

    3.0% click rate for the $20 incentive email vs. 1.1–2.1% for non-incentive emails
    Show reasoning

    The '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' email achieved the highest click rate (3.0%) among valid sends, suggesting incentives drive action once the email is opened. However, the other $20 email ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20') had the lowest open rate (5.0%), so the incentive alone does not guarantee opens — framing matters more for open rates.

    Urgency language ('Last call', 'Last chance') sustains strong open rates in follow-up sends.

    19.1% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' (4,888 sent)
    Show reasoning

    'Last call' achieved a 19.1% open rate on a large list, comparable to the top performers. However, the 'Last chance to share your thoughts' email had 0 recipients sent, so it cannot be evaluated. The single valid urgency email is directionally strong but one data point is insufficient for a strong conclusion.

    Very small send lists (under 200 recipients) produce unreliable open rate benchmarks.

    156 and 95 recipients for the two smallest sends, vs. 4,888–10,608 for the larger campaigns
    Show reasoning

    The emails sent to 156 and 95 recipients show open rates of 20.1% and 16.0% respectively, which are plausible but could reflect list quality differences or small-sample noise rather than subject line effectiveness. These should not be used as benchmarks for broad campaign decisions.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear directional pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates range from emails with ~4 words ('Your insights are needed!') to ~10 words ('Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey')

    Open rates between 16–20% appear across both short and long subject lines, with no consistent advantage for either. The lowest-performing valid email ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20') is mid-length. Length alone is not a differentiating factor here.

    Send timing does not reveal a clear pattern given the data available.

    Top open rates occur across sends dated September 2025, October 2025, and June 2026

    The high-performing emails span multiple months and no two emails share the same send date, making it impossible to isolate a day-of-week or time-of-month effect. The two June 2026 emails with 0 recipients sent cannot be evaluated for timing impact.

    Data quality notes: Two emails ('Last chance to share your thoughts' and 'Reminder: We'd Love Your Feedback on Medallion!') were sent to 0 recipients and are entirely excluded from analysis. Two emails were sent to very small lists (95 and 156), making their open rates statistically unreliable for broad conclusions. Audience sizes vary enormously (95 to 10,608), introducing list-quality and segmentation confounds that make direct open rate comparisons between large and small sends potentially misleading. No subject lines appear to be exact repeats, but the two $20-incentive emails and the two urgency emails are thematically paired and analyzed as such.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 2,010 64.6% 1.8%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit 735 54.7% 2.2%
    You’re So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today 56 53.6% 32.1%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  1,021 48.8% 4.3%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 972 42.8% 1.8%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset cluster around two scenarios: small-list transactional sends (post-event thank-yous, on-demand access notifications) sent to highly engaged sub-audiences, and urgency-driven 'last chance' or 'you're so close' messages sent to mid-size segmented lists. The data consistently shows that personalized, event-proximate emails—those sent immediately after or just before a webinar—outperform broad promotional blasts by 20–40 percentage points in open rate. Importantly, the largest sends (10,000+) almost universally cluster at the bottom of the open-rate rankings, suggesting that audience quality and segmentation matter far more than volume. The priority action is to shrink list sizes through tighter segmentation and lean harder into post-event and urgency follow-up sequences where engagement is demonstrably highest.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and proximity subject lines ('You're So Close', 'Last Chance', 'We Missed You') consistently drive the highest open rates on mid-size lists.

    Top 6 emails by open rate (53–67%) all use urgency, proximity, or personal acknowledgment framing.

    Try this next: Lead every webinar sequence with a 'you're so close' or 'last chance' email framed around the recipient's prior engagement—not a cold invite—and send it to a tightly segmented list of partial completers or engaged non-registrants.

    Post-event and on-demand access subject lines achieve high open AND click rates, unlike most high-open emails that see click drop-off.

    'Demo Day available on demand' achieved 66.7% open rate and 26.7% click rate; 'Thank you for spending time with us' hit 64.3% open and 28.6% click.

    Try this next: Build a dedicated post-event email triggered within 24 hours of webinar end, addressed only to registrants, offering on-demand access—this sub-audience reliably converts at 3–10x the rate of cold invite lists.

    Broad list sends (10,000+ recipients) are structurally correlated with low open rates regardless of subject line quality.

    All 7 emails sent to 10,000+ recipients show open rates between 15.7% and 25.7%, averaging ~21%.

    Try this next: Cap promotional webinar sends at under 3,500 recipients by segmenting on engagement signals (prior opens, product usage tier, role) before sending; use the large list only for the very first cold invite and suppress it from all follow-up sequences.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalization tokens used as the literal subject line text (broken merge tags) appear in two high-volume sends and likely suppressed performance.

    Both sends using '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' in the subject achieved 22.8% and 20.0% open rates on 10,864 and 10,332 recipients respectively.
    Show reasoning

    These open rates are not dramatically below the large-list average (~21%), making it difficult to isolate the token failure as the sole cause. However, a broken first-name token in the subject line (rendering the raw variable syntax) is a known deliverability and trust signal issue. The sample size for clean comparison is insufficient to isolate this effect with certainty.

    Gifting and tangible-incentive subject lines ('coffee kit', 'gift box', 'cocktails') produce mixed results depending on list size and context.

    'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' (735 sent) achieved 54.7% open rate; 'This demo ends with a drink' (2,262 sent) achieved only 10.3%.
    Show reasoning

    When the gifting message is paired with urgency and sent to a warm, smaller audience already in a registration funnel, it performs very well. When sent as a cold invite to a large cold list, the same theme underperforms. 'Crafting better care and cocktails' (2,266 sent) hit only 10.2%, suggesting the novelty angle does not compensate for cold audience targeting.

    AI-themed subject lines as standalone hooks ('How AI is transforming hospital operations', 'AI: Empathetic or Efficient?') perform at or below average for large sends.

    'How AI is transforming hospital operations' (1,616 sent) achieved 31.2%; 'AI: Empathetic or Efficient?' (6,019 sent) achieved 22.9%.
    Show reasoning

    The AI theme does not appear to generate outsized interest relative to comparably-sized sends on credentialing and enrollment topics. 'Executive briefing on credentialing risk & AI adoption' (1,545 sent) at 32.5% performed similarly, suggesting AI as an add-on to core pain-point topics does not clearly lift performance beyond the baseline for these list sizes. Sample is too small to draw a strong conclusion.

    Segmented sends targeting recipients by their specific state-level context ('slowest enrollment timeline states' vs. 'fastest') show a notable performance gap.

    'You're in one of the slowest enrollment timeline states' (272 sent) achieved 36.4% vs. 'You're in one of the fastest enrollment timeline states' (1,032 sent) at 32.0%.
    Show reasoning

    Both emails were sent on the same date (2025-11-20), making this a near-controlled comparison. The smaller, more urgency-relevant segment (slowest states) outperformed the larger, less alarming segment by 4.4 percentage points. The list size difference complicates direct comparison, but the directional signal—that negative urgency framing to a smaller, more targeted segment lifts opens—is consistent with the broader pattern in this dataset.

    No Signal

    Short, direct subject lines (under 50 characters) do not consistently outperform longer descriptive subject lines in this dataset.

    'We missed you!' (11 chars, 54.5% open) vs. '[Watch on demand]: Navigating cost, innovation and credentialing and payer enrollment trends' (91 chars, 40.2% open) — both high performers.

    The top and bottom performers span the full range of subject line lengths. Short lines like 'We missed you!' and 'Demo Day available on demand' perform well, but so do longer lines like the on-demand credentialing webinar. Conversely, short-to-medium lines like 'This demo ends with a drink' (30 chars) hit only 10.3%. Audience segment and send context appear to overwhelm any length effect in this data.

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) cannot be evaluated from this dataset.

    No time-of-day or day-of-week data is available; only calendar dates are provided.

    The data includes send dates but not send times or days of week. Even with dates, the dominant variable appears to be list size and audience segment rather than calendar timing—emails sent on the same date (e.g., three emails on 2025-08-13) show wildly different open rates (66.7%, 64.3%, and 20.5%), confirming that timing is not the differentiating factor here.

    Data quality notes: Several important caveats apply to this analysis. First, list sizes range from 8 to 13,988 recipients, and open rates are heavily correlated with audience size—small lists sent to warm, self-selected audiences (post-event attendees, partial registrants) naturally inflate open rates and are not comparable to cold promotional sends; insights should not be applied uniformly across both contexts. Second, at least four subject lines appear in multiple sends to different segments on different dates (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' appears twice with 1,362 and 1,909 recipients; 'Thank you for spending time with us' appears twice with 14 and 93 recipients; 'Executive briefing on credentialing risk & AI adoption' appears three times; 'You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality' appears three times), meaning some apparent 'subject line patterns' may actually reflect audience segment differences rather than wording effects. Third, two sends contain broken personalization tokens in the subject line field, indicating a production quality issue that may affect deliverability benchmarks for those sends. Fourth, with 65 emails total and many sub-segments, no single subject line variable can be isolated cleanly—list composition and send context are likely the dominant variables throughout.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 833 69.3% 4.0%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit 558 61.8% 5.4%
    Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit 183 58.5% 9.3%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 734 56.0% 6.4%
    Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere  120 55.8% 12.5%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing webinar emails in this dataset cluster around two distinct contexts: transactional post-event follow-ups (thank-you and on-demand access emails) and urgency-driven pre-event reminders with a tangible incentive (coffee kit, gift box). The highest open rates consistently belong to small-list transactional sends, but even at scale, the 'You're So Close' urgency frame with a physical gift drove 69% opens on 833 recipients — suggesting the incentive-plus-urgency formula scales. Conversely, vague curiosity or cocktail-themed novelty subject lines ('This demo ends with a drink,' 'Crafting better care and cocktails') landed at the bottom despite large sends. The clearest opportunity is to pair urgency copy with a concrete reward and to treat post-event follow-up as a high-engagement moment worth optimizing further.

    Strong Signal

    Post-event transactional subject lines ('Thank you,' 'We missed you,' 'Watch on demand') achieve the highest open rates in this dataset.

    Top 5 open rates (57.9%–80%) all belong to post-event or on-demand access emails.

    Try this next: Invest in post-event email sequences as a primary engagement driver — A/B test subject line variants in the thank-you and on-demand access sends where engagement is already highest.

    Urgency framing ('Last Chance,' 'So Close,' '⏰') combined with a concrete incentive outperforms urgency alone at scale.

    'You're So Close—Claim your coffee kit' (558 sent, 61.8%) vs. '⏰ Last Chance! Time is running out' (4,014 sent, 39.6%) — a 22-point gap.

    Try this next: Always pair urgency subject lines with a named tangible incentive (gift, kit, resource) rather than relying on countdown language alone.

    Novelty or cocktail-themed subject lines ('This demo ends with a drink,' 'Crafting better care and cocktails') correlate with the lowest open rates.

    'This demo ends with a drink' (806 sent): 19.4% open rate; 'Crafting better care and cocktails': 20.5% — both in the bottom 5.

    Try this next: Retire cocktail/novelty hooks for this audience. Replace with value-explicit subject lines describing what attendees will learn or receive.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalization tokens that failed to render ('[firstname]' fallback visible in subject) did not tank open rates but represent a deliverability and brand risk.

    Two emails with broken tokens (e.g., "{{ personalization_token('contact.firstname', 'Time is running out') }}") averaged 35.8% open rate — near the mid-pack median.
    Show reasoning

    The broken token emails were sent to very large lists (4,183 and 3,962 recipients) and performed around the dataset median, so the rendering failure did not dramatically suppress opens. However, this is a small two-email sample and the audiences may have been highly intent-driven regardless of subject line quality.

    Segmented or smaller list sends consistently show higher open rates, but large-list sends above 800 can still exceed 50% with the right hook.

    Emails sent to fewer than 20 recipients average ~72% open rate; emails sent to 500–900 recipients range from 41% to 69%.
    Show reasoning

    Small transactional lists (4–23 recipients) almost always top the open rate rankings, which is expected due to high prior engagement and selection bias. However, 'You're So Close—Elevate is less than a month away!' (833 sent, 69.3%) and 'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' (558 sent, 61.8%) show the incentive-urgency formula can overcome list-size dilution. The pattern is directional but confounded by audience intent differences.

    '[You're invited]' bracket framing in subject lines performs solidly at scale but not exceptionally.

    Three '[You're invited]' emails on lists of 57–4,310 averaged 40.7% open rate — above the dataset median but not top-quartile.
    Show reasoning

    The bracket framing appears in multiple sends and consistently lands in the 38–45% range. It outperforms generic descriptive lines at scale but is clearly below transactional and incentive-urgency emails. The pattern is consistent enough to suggest the framing adds modest value for cold or warm promotional sends.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines (under ~50 characters) do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Top-ranked email 'Demo Day available on demand' is 30 chars; but 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' (54 chars) scored 56% on 734 sends.

    High-performing emails span a wide range of lengths — from very short transactional lines to medium-length descriptive ones. There is no consistent directional pattern between subject line character count and open rate after controlling for email type (transactional vs. promotional) and list size.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not show a clear pattern in this dataset.

    High open rates appear across multiple months: August 2025 (80%), November 2025 (77.8%), May 2026 (61.8%), and June 2026 (58.3%).

    Top-performing emails are distributed across July 2025 through June 2026 with no concentration around specific months or apparent seasonal trend. Send date metadata lacks day-of-week information, making timing analysis impossible beyond month-level, which shows no signal.

    Data quality notes: Several critical caveats apply: (1) List size varies enormously — from 4 to 5,165 recipients — which heavily influences open rate reliability; emails sent to fewer than 20 recipients (which dominate the top of the open rate rankings) reflect tiny samples and high selection bias, not broadly applicable benchmarks. (2) The subject line 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' appears twice (sent to 734 and 931 recipients on different dates), and '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation...' appears twice as well — these may be resends to non-openers or different segments, inflating apparent pattern consistency. (3) 'What's next after Elevate 2025?' also appears twice with very different list sizes (7 vs. 119) and open rates (71.4% vs. 38.7%), illustrating how audience segment quality dominates subject line effects. (4) Two emails contain unrendered HubSpot personalization tokens in the subject line, indicating a QA failure that should be corrected. (5) No day-of-week, industry segment, or A/B test flag data is available, limiting causal inference throughout.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon 74 73.0% 14.9%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding 70 71.4% 1.4%
    New fields now live for provider, group, facility, and practice location profiles  103 61.2% 1.0%
    Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience 95 59.0% 1.1%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing 89 58.4% 3.4%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset share two traits: they communicate urgency or direct relevance to the recipient's workflow (compliance deadlines, required field changes, action items) and they target smaller, more targeted segments. Emails sent to under 130 recipients consistently outperform the large-list sends, suggesting tighter audience segmentation is more effective than broad blasts. The $43M fundraise emails — the only pure company-news sends — underperformed compared to product and compliance announcements, indicating this audience responds to operational value over brand milestones. Going forward, prioritize urgency-framed, workflow-relevant subjects over company-news angles, and consider segmenting large lists before sending.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and compliance-action framing in subject lines drives higher open rates than feature announcements.

    78.6% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' vs. 45.3% average for $43M raise emails

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with compliance deadlines, required actions, or workflow changes rather than feature names or company news.

    Pure company-news subjects (funding announcements) are the lowest-performing category in this dataset.

    The four $43M raise variants averaged 36.3% open rate, the lowest cluster in the dataset.

    Try this next: If sending funding or company-news emails, pair the announcement with a direct customer benefit or action item in the subject line rather than leading with the dollar figure.

    Smaller, more targeted audience segments produce noticeably higher open rates.

    Emails sent to ≤130 recipients averaged ~60% open rate; emails sent to ≥198 recipients averaged ~42% open rate.

    Try this next: Segment announcement lists by role, product usage, or affected feature rather than blasting the full customer base.

    High click rates correlate with transactional or participatory emails (feedback requests, advisory board invites) rather than feature announcements.

    'Thank You + Quick Feedback Request' achieved 64.3% click rate; 'Welcome + Member Kit for Advisory Board' achieved 42.9% — both far above the ~3–7% typical for feature emails.

    Try this next: For emails where click-through matters, frame the CTA as a personal invite or direct request rather than a generic 'learn more' about a product update.

    Moderate Signal

    'New Analytic reports' subject line repeated across multiple sends shows diminishing returns over time.

    First 'New Analytic reports' send (n=70) opened at 71.4%; later sends of the same framing dropped to 52.5% and 38.9%.
    Show reasoning

    The same 'New Analytic reports are now available for…' template was used at least four times. The earliest large send hit 71.4%, but subsequent sends of the same format trended lower, suggesting subject-line fatigue or audience overlap. However, segment sizes differed across sends, making a clean comparison difficult.

    'Coming soon' preview emails do not consistently outperform 'Now live' launch emails.

    'Coming soon: Provider profile redesign' opened at 49.2% vs. 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' at 59.0% for similar audience sizes.
    Show reasoning

    For the provider profile sequence, the 'Now live' email outperformed its 'Coming soon' preview. However, this is only one paired example in the data, and other 'Coming soon' emails (e.g., payer names) performed comparably to their live counterparts. The pattern is directional but not conclusive.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear correlation with open rate in this dataset.

    Top openers range from 7 words ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request') to 14 words ('New fields now live for provider, group, facility, and practice location profiles').

    Both short and long subject lines appear across the full range of open rates. The highest open rates include mid-length and long subjects alike. There is no consistent pattern supporting a length recommendation from this data.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not show a clear pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are spread across October, November, January, February, March, and April with no clustering.

    Top-performing emails are distributed across weekdays and months without a discernible seasonal or day-of-week cluster. The dataset lacks send-time granularity, and the sample of 31 emails is too small to isolate timing as a variable independent of content and audience size.

    Data quality notes: Several caveats apply: (1) One email was sent to a single recipient and recorded 100% open rate — this is a statistical artifact and should be excluded from pattern analysis. (2) Audience sizes vary dramatically (1 to 547), which conflates list quality and segmentation effects with subject line performance; smaller lists likely reflect higher-intent or more curated segments. (3) At least four emails share the 'New Analytic reports are now available for…' subject line template across different sends and segments, making it difficult to isolate subject wording as the variable. (4) The $43M fundraise was sent on the same date across multiple segments (2025-08-18), creating potential overlap or fatigue effects that are hard to disentangle. (5) With only 31 emails total, most sub-group comparisons involve fewer than 5 data points, so all 'moderate' findings should be treated as directional hypotheses rather than conclusions.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 864 53.9% 5.9%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 768 53.6% 3.6%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 819 46.9% 4.6%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 834 46.6% 5.2%
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 853 45.7% 3.9%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails consistently pair urgency or exclusivity framing (invitations, final chances, report releases) with concrete financial or operational pain points tied to payer enrollment and provider readiness. Segmented or smaller sends to presumably warmer audiences outperform large cold blasts by 15-25 percentage points in open rate, suggesting list quality and targeting matter more than volume. To replicate success, prioritize invitation-style or report-anchored subject lines that name a specific cost or bottleneck, and reserve broad sends for messages with demonstrated resonance in smaller cohort tests first.

    Strong Signal

    Invitation and urgency framing in subject lines drives the highest open rates.

    53.9% open rate for '[You're invited]' vs. 26.7-30.4% for plain descriptive subjects on similar topics

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with bracketed invitation or urgency cues such as '[You're invited]' or '[Final chance]' when promoting webinars or events, pairing them with a specific financial pain point in the remainder of the subject.

    Smaller, likely warmer audience segments consistently outperform large cold-list sends on identical or similar topics.

    Segments of under ~900 recipients average ~40% open rate vs. ~27% for sends of 2,500+

    Try this next: Prioritize sending content emails to tightly defined, high-fit segments rather than the full list. Test subject lines on smaller warm segments first, then promote winners to broader audiences only if the topic proves resonant.

    Moderate Signal

    Named financial stakes (e.g., '$1M+', 'costs') in subject lines correlate with top open rates.

    53.9% open rate for the subject line naming '$1M+' in costs
    Show reasoning

    The single highest-performing email explicitly quantifies financial impact ('costs some teams $1M+'). Other top performers reference cost or revenue impact ('time to revenue', 'costing you') without a specific number. However, there are too few examples with explicit dollar figures to isolate this variable from the invitation framing also present in that email.

    Report-branded subjects ('[Report]') perform well but inconsistently across list segments.

    45.7% open rate on the best '[Report]' send vs. 19.4% on the worst, same date
    Show reasoning

    The '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' subject was sent to three segments on the same date and produced open rates of 45.7%, 33.0%, and 19.4%. The large spread (26 pp) suggests audience segment quality drives the result more than the subject line itself.

    Coffee-themed or conversational subject lines perform above average but below direct invitation framing.

    45.4% open rate for '☕ Coffee Break?' vs. 53.6% for '[Final chance for coffee]'
    Show reasoning

    Two emails use a coffee theme: the emoji-led '☕ Coffee Break?' (45.4%) and '[Final chance for coffee]' (53.6%). Both outperform the dataset average, but the more urgent '[Final chance]' framing outperforms the softer open-ended question. Sample is only two emails so causality is unclear.

    Hospital-closure urgency themes underperform relative to payer enrollment and revenue-impact themes.

    Hospital-closure emails average ~31% open rate vs. ~44% for payer/revenue-impact emails (comparable segment sizes)
    Show reasoning

    Emails themed around hospital closures ('The hidden crisis behind hospital closures', 'When a local hospital closes, are you ready?') consistently opened in the 25-37% range. Payer enrollment and provider readiness emails on similar-sized lists opened at 37-54%. This suggests the revenue-impact framing resonates more strongly with this audience.

    Click rates are highest when content is specific and actionable (AI topic, data reports, case studies).

    9.0% click rate on 'When hospitals close' (small segment) and 6.0% on 'AI in Credentialing: what to read next'
    Show reasoning

    The highest click rates appear on emails offering a clear next-read or specific report, such as the AI credentialing digest (6.0%) and the hospital-closure email to a small warm segment (9.0%). However, high-open-rate emails don't always produce proportionally high click rates, suggesting open and click drivers are partially independent.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top subject line is 78 characters; high performers range from ~28 to ~78 characters

    High-performing and low-performing emails are spread across short and long subject lines with no consistent directional trend. The top open rates include both one of the longest subjects in the set and mid-length ones, making length a non-predictive variable here.

    Send timing (day of week or time) cannot be assessed from this dataset.

    No send time data available; dates only

    The dataset includes send dates but no send times, and most sends cluster around different campaign themes rather than A/B timing tests. No timing-based pattern can be responsibly extracted.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines were sent to multiple segments on the same date (notably all three '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' sends on 2026-01-21, and duplicate sends for 'When hospitals close', 'Test your operational readiness', and others), making it impossible to isolate subject line effect from audience segment effect. Audience sizes range from 36 to 2,640, introducing significant skew: the four largest sends (2,500-2,640) likely represent cold or broad lists and structurally depress open rates, while sends under 300 likely represent warm or highly targeted segments with inflated open rates. Two emails (sent:45 and sent:36) are too small to draw reliable conclusions from. Comparisons across sends should account for these audience-quality and size differences before attributing performance differences to subject line choices alone.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 159 34.0% 5.0%
    You’re Invited to Dinner @ Juniper and Ivy 176 30.1% 1.7%
    Reserve Your Seat: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Atlanta 136 29.4% 1.5%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 705 28.9% 2.7%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 688 26.2% 3.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing micro event emails combine specificity (named venue, named city, named event) with exclusivity signals like 'Executive Dinner' or branded experience names, and tend to reach smaller, more targeted audiences. Emails sent to fewer than 200 recipients consistently outperform mass sends of 500+ to the same or similar events, suggesting list segmentation quality matters more than scale. The lowest-performing cluster is the Nashville/Tennessee healthcare series from July 2025, which used vague subject lines without venue names and suffered from audience fatigue across multiple sends. Going forward, lead with a specific venue or experience name in the subject line and reserve broader list sends for confirmed high-performing formats only.

    Strong Signal

    Emails naming a specific venue or branded experience in the subject line outperform generic 'exclusive evening' language.

    28.9% average open rate for venue-named subjects vs. ~11% for generic 'Exclusive Evening' subjects

    Try this next: Always include the venue or experience name in the subject line rather than relying on 'exclusive' or 'executive' descriptors alone.

    Smaller, targeted sends (under 200 recipients) consistently achieve higher open rates than large sends of 500+ for equivalent events.

    Sends under 200 average ~26% open rate; sends over 500 average ~16% open rate

    Try this next: Prioritize tight, high-fit segmentation over list size; cap initial sends at 150–200 for premium event invitations and only expand to broader lists if the event has proven appeal.

    Moderate Signal

    Shorter, conversational subject lines like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' perform competitively against longer formal ones.

    23.7–24.5% open rate for the two 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sends
    Show reasoning

    The informal 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' format (26 characters) achieved open rates comparable to the longer, more formal Aquavit and Atlanta dinner subjects. However, only two sends use this format, and they went to mid-sized lists (49 and 274), making it hard to isolate subject length from audience quality. The pattern is directional but not conclusive.

    Urgency-framing subject lines ('Last Chance,' 'Final Seats,' 'Last Call') show mixed results and do not reliably lift performance.

    'Final Seats Remaining' Boston email achieved 25.4% open rate, but 'Last Chance' Nashville emails averaged ~10%
    Show reasoning

    Urgency framing worked well for the Boston healthcare dinner ('Final Seats Remaining' at 25.4%) but failed for Nashville ('Last Chance: Exclusive Evening with Tennessee Healthcare Executives' at 10.0%). The difference appears to be the underlying event appeal and list quality rather than the urgency language itself. Urgency alone cannot rescue a poorly-performing campaign.

    Conference-anchored invitations (e.g., 'During JPM') appear to lift open rates, but sample size is too small to confirm.

    'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' achieved 34.0% on 159 recipients
    Show reasoning

    The JPM-anchored dinner email is the highest-performing large-list send in the dataset. Tying an event to a major industry conference (JPM Healthcare Conference) likely improves relevance for recipients already planning to attend. However, only one email uses this explicit conference-anchor format, so this remains a single-datapoint observation.

    Follow-up sends to the same event degrade open rates, but less than expected when the subject line changes meaningfully.

    Aquavit first send 28.9% (705 recipients) vs. second send 26.2% (688 recipients) — only 2.7 percentage point drop
    Show reasoning

    The Aquavit dinner was sent twice with nearly identical subject lines and saw only a modest open rate decline. The Boston healthcare series also held reasonably well across three sends (20.9%, 25.4%, 19.7%) with varied subject lines. In contrast, the Nashville series degraded sharply. This suggests list overlap and subject line freshness both influence follow-up performance, but the data is too mixed to draw a firm rule.

    Zero-click outcomes on high-open emails suggest CTA placement or link friction may be an issue for some sends.

    4 emails with open rates above 14% recorded 0.0% click rates
    Show reasoning

    The Flour + Water resend (2 recipients, 100% open, 0% click), the Finch dinner (70 recipients, 14.3% open, 0% click), and the Nashville cluster all show opens without clicks. For the 2-recipient send this is noise, but for the 70-recipient Finch send and the Nashville emails (268–542 recipients), zero clicks despite meaningful opens suggests either the CTA was unclear, the RSVP link was broken, or readers opened without intent to act. This is worth auditing but sample overlap with poor-performing campaigns makes causality unclear.

    No Signal

    Send timing (day of week) shows no clear pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates span Monday through Friday with no clustering

    The data does not include day-of-week or time-of-day metadata beyond calendar dates. Even examining dates, top performers are spread across different days of the month with no identifiable timing cluster. No send-timing conclusion is supported by this data.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple sends with different list sizes and dates (e.g., the Aquavit dinner sent twice, the Golden Knights experience sent three times on varying dates, the Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience sent twice, 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent three times) — open rate differences between these duplicate sends likely reflect audience segment quality rather than subject line performance, and they should not be treated as independent A/B tests. The dataset also has significant audience-size skew: send volumes range from 2 to 749, and smaller sends (under 50) produce statistically unreliable rates. The n=2 Flour + Water resend (100% open rate) should be disregarded as a benchmark. With only 31 total emails, many sub-patterns have fewer than 5 data points, limiting statistical confidence across the board.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Your December Roundup ✨ 102 60.8% 3.9%
    Your July Roundup ✨ 114 58.8% 0.9%
    Your September Roundup ✨ 142 58.5% 2.8%
    Your November Roundup ✨ 99 57.6% 6.1%
    Your October Roundup ✨ 138 57.2% 1.5%
    What this tells us

    The 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' format consistently outperforms the 'Medallion Product Update' format, averaging roughly 58-67% open rates versus 45-51% for the rebranded style. The shift to the 'Medallion Product Update' naming coincides with a larger audience size, but even accounting for scale the open rate drop is notable. To maximize opens, the company should reconsider reverting to or testing the personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup' framing. Improving click rates alongside open rates remains a challenge — high opens don't automatically translate to clicks, so CTA clarity within the email body deserves attention.

    Strong Signal

    'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines outperform 'Medallion Product Update' subject lines by roughly 8-12 percentage points in open rate.

    ~58% avg open rate for Roundup format vs ~49% for Product Update format

    Try this next: Revert to the 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject line format or A/B test it against 'Medallion Product Update' on the same send list to isolate the format effect.

    High open rates do not reliably predict high click rates in this dataset.

    'Your June Roundup ✨' had 66.7% open rate but 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Audit the in-email CTA placement and link count for the November Roundup (6.1% CTR) and replicate those content/CTA patterns in future sends.

    The single-recipient 'Your July Roundup ✨' send (0.0% open, 0.0% click) appears to be a test or error send and should be excluded from analysis.

    1 recipient, 0.0% open rate on 2025-08-08 (vs 114 recipients, 58.8% open rate for the same subject on 2025-08-06)

    Try this next: Exclude single-digit recipient sends from performance benchmarking reports to avoid distorting averages.

    Audience size grew significantly over the tracking period, roughly doubling from ~100-150 to ~270-286 recipients.

    From 99-147 recipients (mid-2025) to 270-286 recipients (early-mid 2026)

    Try this next: Segment new vs. tenured subscribers when comparing open rates across time periods to isolate format effects from list-growth dilution.

    Moderate Signal

    Shorter, personalized subject lines ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨') outperform longer branded product lines ('Medallion Product Update - [Month] [Year]').

    ~24 characters for Roundup vs ~34 characters for Product Update subjects
    Show reasoning

    The Roundup subjects are shorter and start with 'Your,' adding a personal pronoun, while Product Update subjects include the brand name and full date. Both length and personalization vary simultaneously, making it impossible to isolate which factor drives the difference from this data alone.

    The November Roundup achieved the highest click rate (6.1%) among all sends with a meaningful sample size.

    6.1% click rate with 99 recipients
    Show reasoning

    November's email stands out for click engagement compared to other Roundup emails (range: 0.0-3.9%) and all Product Update emails (2.5-3.6%). However, with only 99 recipients and no visibility into email body content, it's unclear what drove the higher CTR — this could reflect content, seasonality, or audience composition.

    No Signal

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    Emails sent across multiple months and dates with no consistent day/time metadata provided

    The dataset only includes send dates (not times), and emails are distributed across various weekdays without enough repetition to identify a timing pattern. No timing-based conclusion can be drawn from this data.

    Data quality notes: Several caveats apply: (1) The 'Your June Roundup ✨' email was sent to only 6 recipients and has the highest open rate (66.7%), making it statistically unreliable — small-sample sends should not be used as benchmarks. (2) 'Your July Roundup ✨' appears twice — once as a real send (114 recipients, 2025-08-06) and once as what appears to be a test send (1 recipient, 2025-08-08), inflating the email count artificially. (3) The shift from the Roundup format to the Product Update format coincides with significant audience growth (~100-150 to ~270-286 recipients), meaning format and list composition change simultaneously and cannot be cleanly isolated. (4) No send-time data is available, preventing any time-of-day analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing onboarding emails share a pattern of problem-framing subject lines that name a specific pain point without revealing the solution, driving open rates of 40–56%. Emails that led with vague process themes ('smarter onboarding') or explicit metrics ('64% cost savings') underperformed on opens. The one email that broke the pattern — 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about' — had the highest click rate (18.2%) despite a middling open rate, suggesting that exclusivity framing may drive deeper engagement from the smaller subset who opens. Next time, prioritize problem-naming subject lines to maximize opens, and test exclusivity language ('no one talks about') as a click driver.

    Strong Signal

    Emails with very small send lists (5 or fewer recipients) produce unreliable open rate signals.

    Sends of 1–5 recipients account for 3 of the 8 emails, including the only 0%-open send with n=1.

    Try this next: Enforce a minimum send threshold (e.g., 20+ recipients) before treating open or click rates as valid signals for subject line testing.

    High open rates do not reliably predict high click rates in this dataset.

    The highest-opening email (55.6%) had a 2.8% click rate; the highest-clicking email (18.2%) had only a 27.3% open rate.

    Try this next: Track open rate and click rate as separate optimization targets with different subject line and content strategies rather than optimizing for one as a proxy for the other.

    Moderate Signal

    Problem-framing subject lines that name a specific pain outperform solution- or metric-led subject lines on open rate.

    55.6% open rate for 'The silent slowdown affecting your providers' vs. 25.0% for 'Credential once. Share securely. Save up to 64% on cost.'
    Show reasoning

    The three highest-opening emails all name an unresolved problem ('silent slowdown,' 'smarter onboarding,' 're-verify the same providers') rather than stating a solution or benefit. The metric-forward subject line and the explicit solution framing both landed at 25% or below. The pattern is directional but the sample is small and audience sizes vary significantly across sends.

    Exclusivity framing ('no one talks about') drives the highest click rate even at a middling open rate.

    18.2% click rate for 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about' — 5–6x higher than the next best click rate.
    Show reasoning

    This subject line had only a 27.3% open rate but generated by far the highest click-through rate, suggesting it attracted a highly engaged subset. This could indicate that 'insider knowledge' framing converts openers more effectively, but it is one data point and the send size was only 11 recipients.

    Shorter subject lines (under ~55 characters) tend to achieve higher open rates than longer ones in this dataset.
