    Top 3 subjects by open rate average ~47 characters; the two 25%-open subjects average ~63 characters.
    Show reasoning

    'The silent slowdown affecting your providers' (46 chars) and 'What smarter onboarding looks like' (34 chars) are among the shortest and the best-opening. The longest subject — 'What if credentialing files took 1 day instead of 1 week?' (57 chars) — sits at 25%. The pattern is roughly consistent but sample size is too small to isolate length from wording effects.

    No Signal

    No meaningful send-timing pattern is detectable in this dataset.

    7 of 8 emails sent on 2025-12-09; 1 sent on 2025-12-10.

    Virtually all emails were sent on the same date, making it impossible to identify any day-of-week or time-based open rate differences. The one email sent on a different date (2025-12-10) performed at 25%, indistinguishable from several same-day sends.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is very small (8 emails, 100 total sends across all) and audience sizes range from 1 to 36 recipients per send, creating significant skew — open and click rates for sends under 10 recipients are statistically unreliable and should be treated as directional at best. All 8 emails were sent within a two-day window (Dec 9–10, 2025), eliminating any meaningful send-timing analysis. No subject lines appear to be repeated across multiple sends or segments. Findings should be treated as hypotheses to test at larger scale rather than confirmed conclusions.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 1,817 34.4% 6.8%
    Your insights are needed! 2,276 33.4% 5.9%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 1,638 32.2% 4.4%
    Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments 94 27.7% 2.1%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 7,747 21.4% 4.6%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails combine personalization, an explicit monetary incentive, and a concise, benefit-forward subject line. Emails addressing the recipient directly (by name or with 'your') and pairing that with a $20 reward consistently outperform more generic or highly specific subject lines. The one major exception is the highest-volume send ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' at 21.4% open rate), which underperforms despite featuring the same incentive — suggesting that audience size or segmentation quality matters as much as subject line framing. Future sends should prioritize personalization tokens and tighter audience targeting over broad blasts, even when a strong incentive is present.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalization tokens in the subject line are associated with higher open rates.

    34.4% open rate for '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' vs. 21.4% for 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20'
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing email uses a first-name personalization token alongside the $20 incentive, while the largest send uses nearly identical incentive language without personalization and gets the lowest open rate of the group. However, the audiences differ substantially in size (1,817 vs. 7,747), so list quality and segmentation likely confound the comparison, making this directional rather than conclusive.

    Explicit $20 incentive language in the subject line lifts open rates when paired with personalization or direct address, but not on its own.

    34.4% open rate (personalized + $20) vs. 21.4% (generic + $20)
    Show reasoning

    Two emails feature a $20 incentive: the personalized one achieves the highest open rate in the set, while the generic version achieves the lowest. The incentive alone does not guarantee performance — framing and personalization appear to be necessary co-factors. The large audience size of the lower-performing send (7,747) may also be suppressing its rate.

    Shorter, benefit-focused subject lines outperform longer, topic-specific ones.

    27.7% open rate for a 55-character specific subject vs. 33–34% for shorter, broader subjects
    Show reasoning

    'Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments' is both the longest and most technically specific subject line and has the second-lowest open rate (27.7%). Shorter subjects like 'Your insights are needed!' (29 chars, 33.4%) perform better. However, this email was also sent to only 94 recipients, so niche audience targeting may explain the lower rate as much as subject length.

    Smaller, more targeted audience segments tend to achieve higher open rates than large broad sends.

    The 7,747-recipient send had the lowest open rate (21.4%); four sends under 2,300 recipients all exceeded 32%
    Show reasoning

    Every email sent to fewer than 2,300 recipients achieved an open rate of 32% or higher, while the largest send (7,747) achieved only 21.4%. The two very small sends (37 and 94 recipients) also perform well but are statistically unreliable at those sizes. This pattern is consistent but confounded by the fact that smaller sends may also be better segmented or sent to more engaged sub-audiences.

    Urgency or scarcity framing ('Last call') sustains strong open rates even late in a campaign sequence.

    32.2% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' sent October 7, after earlier sends in the same campaign
    Show reasoning

    This subject line, sent latest in what appears to be a sequential campaign (Sept 17 → Sept 23 → Oct 7), still achieved a 32.2% open rate despite likely overlapping with a fatigued audience. However, without data on overlap between recipient lists, it is unclear whether this reflects scarcity language working or a distinct audience segment.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear, consistent correlation with open rate across this dataset.

    Open rates range from 21.4% to 34.4% with no monotonic relationship to character count

    The highest open rate belongs to a mid-length subject ('{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' ~43 chars), but the lowest belongs to the highest-volume send with a similarly mid-length subject. Length appears secondary to personalization, incentive framing, and audience quality in this dataset. No actionable length rule can be extracted.

    Send timing does not show a clear pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates span dates from September 17 to October 7, 2025 with no day-of-week data available

    The data provides send dates but not send times or days of week in a way that enables pattern detection. The top and bottom performers are only three weeks apart (September 17 vs. September 29), and without day-of-week or time-of-day data, no timing conclusion is supportable.

    Data quality notes: Audience sizes vary enormously across these six sends — from 37 to 7,747 recipients — making direct open-rate comparisons unreliable; rates from the two smallest sends (37 and 94 recipients) are statistically fragile and should be treated as anecdotal. The September 2025 emails ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20,' 'Your insights are needed!,' '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!,' and 'Last call...') appear to be a sequential campaign to overlapping or identical audiences, meaning their rates may reflect list fatigue or de-duplication rather than subject line differences alone. No subject lines appear to be exact repeats, but the incentive theme ($20) appears in two distinct emails, and 'Medallion' branding appears in two others, suggesting campaign clustering rather than fully independent tests.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    A better analytics experience is coming your way 83 60.2% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing product release emails share two traits: they announce that something is already live (rather than previewing upcoming changes), and they use specific, descriptive subject lines that name the exact feature being released. The highest open rate (75%) went to a concise, jargon-free 'now live' announcement sent to a small list, while the two preview-style emails ('coming soon' framing) both generated zero clicks despite solid open rates. The core takeaway is to lead with concrete 'now available' language and include enough specificity that readers immediately understand what changed — save teaser framing for drip campaigns, not release announcements.

    Strong Signal

    'Now live' or 'now available' subject lines outperform preview/teaser framing on open rates and drive all of the clicks.

    8.3% click rate for 'now live' vs. 0.0% for both preview-style emails

    Try this next: Write product release subject lines in the past/present tense and use 'now live' or 'now available' rather than teaser language — save preview framing for pre-launch warming emails only.

    Preview emails with no CTA click activity suggest recipients open out of curiosity but take no action when nothing is yet available to act on.

    0.0% click rate across both preview/teaser emails combined (2 of 2)

    Try this next: Do not send a preview-style release email unless there is a linked resource (e.g., a preview doc, waitlist, or changelog) worth clicking — otherwise, hold the announcement until the feature is live.

    Moderate Signal

    Including the specific feature name in the subject line appears to correlate with higher engagement, but the sample is too small to isolate this cleanly.

    63.4% open rate and 4.9% click rate for the specific 'Provider Summary and Credentialing' subject vs. 60.2% for the vaguer 'better analytics experience' subject
    Show reasoning

    The most specific subject line ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing') outperformed the generic 'better analytics experience' teaser on both opens and clicks. However, the live vs. preview framing differences between these two emails confound the comparison, making it impossible to attribute the difference to specificity alone.

    Smaller audience segments produced higher open rates, but this may reflect list composition rather than a size effect.

    36-recipient email achieved 75% open rate vs. 60.2% for the 83-recipient email
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing email was sent to only 36 recipients while the lowest open rate email went to 83, suggesting the smaller, possibly more targeted list engaged more. However, with only 5 sends and different subject lines and dates, audience size cannot be cleanly isolated as the causal variable.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Subjects range from 36 to 76 characters with no monotonic relationship to open rate

    The top performer (75% open rate) has a short subject (39 chars), but the second-highest (63.4%) has one of the longest subjects in the set. The bottom performer also has the same long subject. Length does not explain the variance here — framing and content specificity are more likely drivers. No actionable length pattern exists in this data.

    Send timing shows no clear day-of-week or cadence pattern that predicts performance.

    Sends span July through September with no clustering around high or low performers

    Emails were sent on dates spread across three months (July 9, July 21, August 5, September 10, September 17) and the performance ranking does not track with recency or any obvious seasonal pattern. Without day-of-week metadata, a send-timing conclusion cannot be drawn from this data.

    Data quality notes: This analysis covers only 5 emails with audience sizes ranging from 36 to 83 recipients — all figures should be treated as directional only, as small denominators cause open and click rates to swing significantly with even one or two additional interactions. The subject line 'New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing' appears twice across two sends (September 10 and September 17) to what appear to be different or overlapping segments (41 and 39 recipients respectively); their performance difference (63.4% vs. 56.4% open rate, 4.9% vs. 0.0% click rate) may reflect segment quality or fatigue from a repeat send rather than any subject line variable. No send-time or day-of-week data was provided, limiting timing analysis.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 178 74.2% 5.1%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit 130 61.5% 13.1%
    Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit 52 57.7% 11.5%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar 51 56.9% 3.9%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 140 51.4% 7.9%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing emails in this dataset share two clear traits: urgency-framed subject lines (last chance, time running out, so close) and tangible incentive offers (coffee kits, gift boxes), with these two patterns often combining to push open rates above 55%. Notably, the highest open rates cluster around segmented or targeted sends with smaller audience sizes, suggesting that list precision matters alongside subject line craft. Emails promoting the Elevate conference franchise and Demo Day events consistently outperform generic platform demo invites, indicating that branded event identity drives engagement. The main opportunity is pairing urgency copy and physical incentives — currently used successfully in smaller sends — with larger broadcast sends to test whether the lift is replicable at scale.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency-framing in subject lines ('Last Chance,' 'So Close,' time pressure) is associated with above-average open rates.

    Top 7 emails by open rate (57.7%–74.2%) all use urgency or proximity language.

    Try this next: Open every webinar or event reminder sequence with an urgency-anchored subject line for at least the final two sends before the event date.

    Physical incentive offers (coffee kit, gift box) paired with registration drive very high click rates as well as opens.

    'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' achieved 61.5% open rate AND 13.1% click rate on 130 recipients.

    Try this next: Incorporate a named physical incentive (e.g., coffee kit, branded box) into at least one mid-funnel webinar reminder email, especially for registration campaigns where you need both an open and a click.

    Emails sent to very small segments (under 20 recipients) inflate apparent open rates and should not be used as performance benchmarks.

    5 of the top 10 open-rate emails were sent to 18 or fewer recipients.

    Try this next: Segment your performance analysis by send size (e.g., under 50 vs. 50–300 vs. 300+) before drawing subject line or content conclusions.

    Branded event name in subject line ('Elevate,' 'Demo Day') is associated with higher open rates than generic platform or topic-led subjects.

    Elevate/Demo Day branded emails average ~43% open rate across all sizes; non-branded webinar subjects average ~25% open rate.

    Try this next: When launching new webinar series, invest early in establishing a consistent event brand name (like 'Elevate' or 'Demo Day') and use it explicitly in every email subject line rather than varying the descriptor each time.

    Moderate Signal

    Shorter, punchier subject lines (under ~60 characters) tend to outperform long descriptive ones.

    The 8 emails with open rates above 50% have an average subject length of roughly 45 characters, versus ~70 for emails below 35%.
    Show reasoning

    Subjects like 'We missed you at Elevate!' (28 chars), 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' (43 chars), and 'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' (38 chars) all perform strongly, while longer subjects like '[RSVP now] Join healthcare leaders shaping the future of value-based care' (73 chars) and 'Are you registered? Credentialing & enrollment automation w/a twist' (67 chars) land in the bottom half. However, the high-open short-subject emails also tend to be smaller sends, so audience targeting confounds this signal.

    Broken personalization tokens in subject lines do not appear to hurt open rates meaningfully.

    Two emails with unrendered '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' prefixes still achieved 31.2% and 30.6% open rates on ~1,000-recipient sends.
    Show reasoning

    These two sends performed comparably to other large-list Elevate countdown emails (e.g., 'Rethink revenue cycle performance at Elevate 2025' at 30.0%), suggesting the remainder of the subject line carried the weight. This is a moderate finding because there are only two data points with broken tokens and no clean A/B control.

    Post-event 'missed you' and on-demand watch emails show mixed results and no consistent engagement lift.

    'We missed you at Elevate!' opened at 66.7% (n=12) but 'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand' opened at only 17.6% (n=17).
    Show reasoning

    The high performer is a tiny send of 12 which is statistically unreliable. The larger comparable sends in the post-event category (17–18 recipients) show 17.6%–50%, a wide range. There is not enough volume or consistency to conclude that post-event follow-up emails outperform or underperform the campaign average.

    No Signal

    Send timing patterns (day of week, time of day) cannot be assessed from this dataset.

    No time-of-day or day-of-week data is present in the dataset.

    Only send dates are available, not send times or days of week. Even date-level analysis is confounded by the fact that high-volume sends and small targeted sends happen on overlapping dates. No timing conclusion is supported.

    Personalized geographic segmentation ('fastest enrollment timeline states' vs. 'slowest') shows no meaningful open rate difference.

    'Fastest enrollment timeline states' (n=90) opened at 46.7% vs. 'slowest' (n=40) at 32.5% — but audience sizes and list quality differ.

    The two geo-segmented sends differ in size (90 vs. 40) and the 'fastest' version may have been sent to a warmer segment. There is no clean A/B pairing here, so the 14-point gap cannot be attributed to the geographic angle in the subject line.

    Data quality notes: This dataset has significant audience-size skew: sends range from 1 to 1,386 recipients, and 12 of the 59 emails were sent to fewer than 20 people. Small-send open rates (often 40–100%) are likely driven by audience warmth rather than subject line quality and should not be directly compared to large-list sends. Several subject lines appear to be resends or segmented variants of the same campaign (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' sent twice on different dates to 140 and 175 recipients; 'Executive briefing on credentialing risk & AI adoption' sent to 62 and 231 recipients on consecutive days), which means some 'data points' are not independent experiments. Two subject lines contain unrendered HubSpot personalization tokens, indicating deliverability or template errors. The '\ xa0' prefix on several subjects suggests encoding artifacts that may affect rendering in some email clients.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 189 60.3% 5.3%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 169 56.2% 9.5%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 154 49.4% 9.1%
    ☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue? 209 47.4% 6.7%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 162 44.4% 8.0%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset consistently combine urgency or specificity in the subject line with moderate-to-large audience segments (100–200 recipients), suggesting these messages reached a well-matched audience rather than a cold or exhausted list. Emails using bracketed labels like '[You're invited]' or '[Final chance]' and those referencing concrete financial stakes (e.g., '$1M+', 'time to revenue') achieved both strong opens and clicks, while purely educational or report-style subjects tended to open well but underperform on clicks. The key lever is pairing a high-curiosity or urgency-framed subject with a clear, action-oriented CTA — emails that did both topped 9% click rates. Future sends should retire vague 'why X is breaking down' framings in favor of outcome-specific language tied to a next step.

    Strong Signal

    Bracketed urgency labels in subject lines drive the highest combined open and click rates.

    9.5% click rate for '[Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue'

    Try this next: Use bracketed urgency or invitation labels (e.g., '[Final chance]', '[You're invited]') in subject lines for high-priority sends, especially when paired with a specific outcome like 'time to revenue'.

    Subjects referencing specific financial or operational stakes outperform vague 'why X is broken' framings on clicks.

    'State-by-state payer delays and what they're costing you' achieved 9.1% clicks vs. 0.9% for 'Why specialty care access is breaking down'

    Try this next: Replace 'Why X is happening' subject framings with 'What X is costing you' or 'How X impacts your [metric]' to convert openers into clickers.

    Emails sent to smaller, likely more targeted segments (under 50 recipients) show inflated open rates that are not meaningful benchmarks.

    Emails with 2–3 recipients hit 66.7%–100% open rates, vs. 25–47% for sends of 150–850

    Try this next: Exclude sends under 30 recipients from open-rate benchmarking and treat them as test rows only.

    The '[Report]' label in subject lines opens adequately but consistently underdelivers on clicks.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment...' click rates: 1.9%, 1.2%, and 2.3% across three sends

    Try this next: Reframe report emails with an action-oriented subject (e.g., 'What the 2026 credentialing data means for your team') and include a specific CTA like a benchmark comparison or worksheet rather than a generic download link.

    Moderate Signal

    Short, conversational or pun-style subjects ('coffee break', 'a latte') yield solid opens but not exceptional ones relative to more specific subjects.

    '☕ Coffee Break?' opened at 47.4% (n=209); 'A latte teams are dealing with...' opened at only 25.8% (n=840)
    Show reasoning

    The coffee-themed emails span a wide open rate range (25.8%–47.4%), and the largest send ('A latte') performed worst among them, suggesting audience fatigue or list quality differences rather than a consistent subject-style effect. The pattern is directional but too inconsistent across the three instances to call strong.

    'AI in Credentialing' content achieved the highest click rate in the dataset despite a small audience.

    13.6% click rate (n=22) for 'AI in Credentialing: what to read next'
    Show reasoning

    This email's 13.6% click rate is the highest in the dataset, but the audience of only 22 recipients makes it statistically unreliable — a single additional click or non-click would shift the rate substantially. The result is intriguing but cannot be treated as a strong signal without a larger send.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear relationship with open or click performance in this dataset.

    Top-performing subjects range from ~45 to ~90 characters with no monotonic trend

    High performers include both shorter subjects ('Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here's what the data says') and longer ones ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)'). Without a consistent length pattern across top vs. bottom performers, length alone is not a meaningful lever here.

    Send timing (day or month) does not produce a clear performance pattern in this dataset.

    Top click rates appear in May 2026, July 2025, October 2025, and November 2025 — no single period dominates

    High-click emails are distributed across multiple months and no single send date clusters with consistently better results. The dataset lacks send-time (hour) data entirely, and date-level patterns are confounded by audience size and subject line differences, making timing conclusions unreliable.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple rows with different sent volumes (2–213 recipients), indicating the same email was split across segments or tested on sub-lists — these should not be treated as independent experiments. The two top open-rate rows (100% and 66.7%) were sent to only 2 and 3 recipients respectively and are almost certainly test sends; they distort any aggregate open-rate averages and should be excluded from benchmarking. Audience size varies dramatically (2 to 840), meaning raw open and click rates are not directly comparable across rows without weighting by volume. No send-time (hour of day) or day-of-week data is available, limiting timing analysis to month-level observations only.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
     $43M raised to help healthcare organizations scale faster 170 45.9% 2.4%
    A refreshed analytics experience is coming next week 59 45.8% 0.0%
    New feature: Track closed practice locations in platform 67 43.3% 3.0%
    $43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse 67 17.9% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset tend to be highly targeted sends to very small audiences (1–3 recipients), which inflates their open and click rates to 100% and makes them unreliable benchmarks. Among emails sent to statistically meaningful audiences (20+ recipients), the strongest performers share urgency or action-oriented framing ('Action required,' 'Coming soon,' direct fee announcements) and concrete feature specificity in the subject line. Fundraising announcement emails consistently underperformed relative to other announcement types, especially as audience size grew. To improve performance, prioritize operationally urgent or change-alerting subject lines over company news, and avoid reusing the same subject line across segmented sends without testing.

    Strong Signal

    Fundraising announcement emails underperform other announcement types, especially at scale.

    The '$43M raised' email sent to 67 recipients had only a 17.9% open rate, the lowest in the dataset.

    Try this next: Separate company milestone announcements from product/operational update sends, and consider whether your announcement list is the right audience for funding news.

    Sending the same subject line to multiple segments produces inconsistent open rates, suggesting audience fit matters more than subject wording alone.

    'New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing' got 48.0% (25 recipients) vs. 33.3% (15 recipients) on a send one week later.

    Try this next: When re-sending or segmenting the same announcement, track segment-level open rates separately and use them to refine your list targeting rather than attributing performance solely to subject line copy.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and action-oriented subject line framing correlates with higher open rates among larger sends.

    53.8% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' vs. 17.9%–45.9% for fundraising announcements at comparable or larger send sizes
    Show reasoning

    Emails framed around required actions, upcoming changes, or imminent deadlines ('Action required,' 'Coming soon,' 'Now live,' 'Required fields') clustered in the 42–63% open rate range for sends of 13–48 recipients. Fundraising emails sent to larger lists landed at 14–46%. The sample sizes vary enough that this is directional rather than conclusive, but the pattern is consistent.

    Subject lines that name a specific feature, product change, or regulatory event outperform generic or company-news subjects.

    Average open rate ~49% for specific feature/change subjects vs. ~31% for '$43M raised' subjects across matched send sizes
    Show reasoning

    Subjects like 'New Analytic reports are now available for New Payer Enrollment TATs' (56.5%), 'Introducing a new passthrough fee: CAQH Roster Fee' (54.0%), and 'Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility' (50.0%) all outperformed funding news at similar send sizes. The audience segments differ, so this is directional.

    Emails with non-zero click rates tend to include concrete product interactions or tangible next steps.

    6 of 29 emails had click rates above 0%; all involved product features, API access, or enrollment record changes rather than informational announcements.
    Show reasoning

    'Generate API keys directly in Medallion' had the highest click rate (8.3%), followed by 'Now live: Standard payer names required' (3.3%), 'New feature: Track closed practice locations' (3.0%), and the fundraising email (2.4%). Purely informational announces — analytics reports, upcoming changes — consistently had 0% click rates. This suggests CTAs only convert when there is a direct, in-product action available.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Open rates range from 17.9% to 63.3% across both short and long subject lines with no consistent length-based grouping.

    Short subjects like 'Unlocking faster Payer Enrollment follow-up' (50.0%) and long ones like 'Coming soon: Standard payer names will be required for all enrollment records' (63.3%) both perform well. Similarly, both short and long subjects appear at low open rates. No length-based pattern is evident.

    Send timing (day or month) does not show a reliable pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are distributed across October 2025 through July 2026 with no clustering by month or apparent day-of-week pattern.

    The data lacks send-time granularity (no hour or day-of-week field), and the date range spans many months with high performers spread throughout. Without day-of-week data and with confounding variables like audience segment and subject framing, no timing conclusion is supportable.

    Data quality notes: Three emails were sent to only 1–3 recipients and achieved 100% open/click rates, which are statistical artifacts of tiny sample sizes and should be excluded from any benchmarking. Many subject lines appear in multiple rows representing separate sends to different audience segments (e.g., '$43M raised' appears 4 times, 'New Analytic reports' variants appear across 8 rows), making it difficult to isolate subject line quality from audience-fit effects. Audience sizes range from 1 to 170, creating significant skew; patterns are most reliable for the cluster of emails sent to 20–67 recipients. No send-time or day-of-week data is available, precluding timing analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Dinner @ Stillwell's ? 55 40.0% 5.5%
    You’re Invited to Dinner @ Juniper and Ivy 55 30.9% 3.6%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 135 30.4% 0.7%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 138 27.5% 2.2%
    Dinner @ Stillwell's Tonight? 77 26.0% 3.9%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this dataset are almost exclusively small-list, experiential invitations tied to specific named venues or live events, with the highest open rates clustering around intimate dinners at recognizable restaurants and sports/entertainment experiences. Casual, conversational subject lines (e.g., 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') and highly specific venue or event names outperform generic urgency-driven copy. Notably, the highest open rates occur on lists of under 55 recipients, suggesting that tightly targeted audience selection may matter as much as subject line copy. To improve future performance, prioritize named-venue specificity, keep lists small and highly qualified, and lean into casual phrasing over formal 'exclusive executive' language.

    Strong Signal

    Subject lines referencing a specific named venue or event property drive higher open rates than generic dinner or event labels.

    All 6 emails with open rates above 30% name a specific venue (Flour + Water, Stillwell's, Aquavit, Table) or branded event (Golden Knights Experience).

    Try this next: Always include the specific venue or event name in the subject line rather than relying on 'exclusive dinner' or role-based descriptors alone.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, conversational subject lines with venue names outperform formal 'exclusive' framing.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' achieved 40.0% open rate vs. 13.3% for 'A Seat at the Table: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Atlanta' sent to the same size list.
    Show reasoning

    Emails using informal phrasing like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' and direct venue references consistently rank in the top half of open rates, while emails using phrases like 'Exclusive,' 'A Seat at the Table,' or 'Reserve Your Seat' cluster in the bottom half. However, the sample is small and list sizes differ, so this is directional rather than conclusive.

    Emails sent to very small lists (under 30 recipients) show higher open rates on average than those sent to large lists (100+).

    Emails sent to fewer than 30 recipients average ~34% open rate; emails sent to 100+ recipients average ~20% open rate.
    Show reasoning

    The top 7 emails by open rate were all sent to 55 or fewer recipients. The four emails sent to 100+ recipients all fall below 31% open rate, with most under 25%. This could reflect better audience targeting on smaller sends rather than list size itself causing higher opens, so causality is unclear.

    Urgency or 'last call' framing in subject lines does not reliably lift open rates in this dataset.

    'Last Call' and 'Final Seats' variants average 26.8% open rate vs. the overall dataset average of ~22%.
    Show reasoning

    'Final Seats Remaining' (23.4%), 'Final Call' (20.6%), 'Last Chance' (13.2% and 19.1%), and 'Last Call' (33.3%) show inconsistent results. The one strong performer ('Last Call: Join us for Medallion's Las Vegas Golden Knights Experience' at 33.3%) was sent to only 24 recipients about a high-interest live event, suggesting the event type rather than urgency language drove performance.

    Sports and live entertainment event invitations drive strong open rates, but the sample is too small to generalize.

    Golden Knights Experience emails average 36.1% open rate across 4 sends; Wizard of Oz Sphere emails average only 15.5% across 2 sends.
    Show reasoning

    The Golden Knights Experience performed exceptionally well, but the Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience underperformed despite a similar format and small list sizes. This suggests event appeal or audience-event fit matters more than the live-event format itself. Only 6 event emails exist in the dataset, limiting conclusions.

    Repeat sends to the same or overlapping audiences for the same event show diminishing open rates over time.

    Aquavit NY first send: 27.5% (138 sent, Sept 4); second send: 30.4% (135 sent, Sept 10) — rates held, but Golden Knights 'Tomorrow' reminder dropped from 56.2% to 37.5% to 33.3%.
    Show reasoning

    For the Golden Knights campaign, open rates declined from 56.2% on the first send to 37.5% and 33.3% on follow-up sends. The Aquavit sends were nearly identical and held relatively stable, possibly because they targeted slightly different segments. The pattern is mixed but suggests follow-up sends to the same list yield diminishing returns.

    JPM-conference-tied dinner invitations outperform non-conference dinners when sent to small, targeted lists.

    'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' achieved 55.0% and 28.0% open rates across two sends.
    Show reasoning

    Both JPM-tied sends performed above the dataset median, with the smaller resend to 20 recipients hitting 55%. However, there are only two data points and the audience was presumably pre-qualified conference attendees, so the conference context and audience quality are confounded.

    No Signal

    Shorter, punchier subject lines do not consistently outperform longer descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Top subject line lengths range from 19 characters ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') to 84 characters ('Don't Miss Medallion's Power Play: A Las Vegas Golden Knights Experience').

    Both very short and moderately long subject lines appear at the top of the open rate rankings, and both appear at the bottom. There is no clear length threshold that separates high from low performers in this data. Length does not appear to be a meaningful predictor here.

    Send timing (day of week or month) shows no consistent pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates are spread across dates ranging from July 2025 to May 2026 with no clustering by weekday or month.

    Without day-of-week data and with sends spread across nearly a year, it is not possible to identify a timing pattern. The data does not support any send-timing recommendation.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 29 emails, many sent to very small lists (5–55 recipients), which makes most open and click rates statistically unreliable — a single open on a 9-person list moves the rate by 11 percentage points. Several subject lines appear across multiple sends with different list sizes and dates (e.g., 'A Seat at the Table: Aquavit' sent twice, 'In Vegas? Golden Knights' sent twice, 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent twice, 'JPM Flour + Water' sent twice), suggesting segmented or re-targeted sends rather than independent experiments — these cannot be treated as independent data points. Large-list sends (100+ recipients) are almost certainly to broader, less-qualified audiences than small-list sends, which severely confounds any open-rate comparison across list sizes. Click rate data shows many zeros and should be interpreted with caution given the small absolute numbers involved.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Medallion Product Update - February 2026 83 43.4% 0.0%
    Medallion Product Update - May 2026 95 43.2% 0.0%
    Medallion Product Update - March 2026 86 39.5% 0.0%
    Medallion Product Update - April 2026 89 36.0% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Newsletter emails in this dataset consistently achieve strong open rates (38–52%) regardless of format, suggesting a highly engaged subscriber base. The monthly 'Roundup' emails with sparkle emojis tend to slightly outperform the 'Product Update' branded emails, but both formats perform well above typical B2B benchmarks. The critical gap is click-throughs: nearly all emails record 0% click rates, meaning opens are not converting to action. The priority improvement is adding clear, compelling CTAs to drive clicks, since the audience is clearly willing to open.

    Strong Signal

    Click-through rates are near zero across virtually all emails, representing the primary performance gap.

    9 out of 11 emails have a 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Add at least one prominent, specific CTA button or link to every email — model it on the July and October Roundup emails, which were the only two to generate any clicks.

    Moderate Signal

    Monthly roundup emails with '✨' in the subject line achieve slightly higher open rates than plain product update emails.

    Average open rate: ~43.5% for Roundup emails vs ~40.5% for Product Update emails
    Show reasoning

    The top 3 open rates all belong to 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' emails (51.7%, 45.5%, 44.2%), while 'Medallion Product Update' emails cluster between 36–43.4%. However, the Roundup emails have much smaller send lists (26–44) vs Product Update emails (83–95), so the difference may reflect audience composition rather than subject line appeal.

    Subject line wording pattern: personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup' framing outperforms company-branded 'Medallion Product Update' framing at the top of the open rate range.

    Top open rate 51.7% (Roundup) vs top Product Update open rate 43.4%
    Show reasoning

    'Your' framing is more personal and reader-centric, which may explain the edge. That said, the two email types also go to different-sized (and possibly different-quality) segments, making a clean comparison impossible from this data alone.

    The July Roundup had the highest click rate (6.5%) among all emails with meaningful send volume.

    6.5% click rate on 31 sends
    Show reasoning

    'Your July Roundup ✨' generated 6.5% clicks vs 0% for most others, suggesting its content or layout included something more actionable. Unfortunately, without content-level data, it's impossible to isolate exactly what drove the clicks. The small send list (31) is also a caveat.

    Audience size (number sent) has grown significantly for Product Update emails but not Roundup emails, suggesting different or growing segments.

    Product Update sends range from 83–95; Roundup sends range from 26–44
    Show reasoning

    The two email types are clearly going to different audiences or the product update list has grown faster. This means open rate comparisons between the two formats are confounded by potential differences in audience quality or engagement history.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not appear to correlate with open rate performance.

    Subject lengths range from ~22 to ~38 characters with no consistent open-rate trend

    Both short ('Your July Roundup ✨') and longer ('Medallion Product Update - February 2026') subjects appear across the full range of open rates. There is no visible pattern linking character count to performance in this dataset.

    Send timing (day of week or month) shows no clear pattern in this data.

    Top open rates span January, August, October, March, and June sends

    High open rates appear across multiple months and different points in the year. Without time-of-day data and with only 11 emails spread across roughly 12 months, no send-timing pattern can be identified here.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 11 emails, limiting statistical confidence for all findings. Critically, the two email formats ('Roundup' and 'Product Update') are sent to different audience sizes (26–44 vs 83–95 recipients respectively), which likely reflects different or segmented lists — making open rate comparisons between formats unreliable. The 'Your June Roundup ✨' email was sent to only 1 recipient and should be treated as a test or error send, not a real data point. No subject lines are repeated across sends, so there are no A/B or resend duplicates to flag.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 486 33.1% 6.2%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 457 29.8% 4.2%
    Your insights are needed! 573 26.0% 6.5%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 1,179 19.9% 4.4%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails share two visible traits: personalization (using the recipient's first name) and a concrete monetary incentive ($20) called out directly in the subject line. The highest open rate email combined both of these elements and was sent to a mid-sized list, suggesting these tactics drive above-average engagement. Urgency framing ('Last call') also performed well among larger sends. Future survey emails should lead with personalization and explicit incentive value in the subject line, and consider urgency language for follow-up sends.

    Moderate Signal

    Explicit $20 incentive in the subject line is associated with higher open rates.

    33.1% open rate for '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' vs. 19.9% for 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20'
    Show reasoning

    Two emails mention the $20 incentive, but they perform very differently (33.1% vs. 19.9%). The top performer pairs the incentive with first-name personalization and a smaller list, while the lower performer sent to 1,179 recipients. The incentive alone doesn't guarantee lift — its placement alongside personalization and list size differences make it hard to isolate the incentive effect alone.

    First-name personalization in the subject line is associated with the highest open rate in this dataset.

    33.1% open rate — the only email using first-name personalization, outperforming all others.
    Show reasoning

    Only one email uses '{{ contact.firstname }}' in the subject line and it achieved the highest open rate at 33.1%. However, it also had a relatively small send list (486) and combined personalization with an incentive mention, so the personalization effect cannot be isolated with confidence from this single data point.

    Urgency framing ('Last call') drove strong opens on a large list.

    29.8% open rate on a 457-recipient send — the second-best rate on a list larger than 100.
    Show reasoning

    The 'Last call' subject line achieved the second-highest open rate and was sent to a comparably sized list as the top performer, suggesting urgency language is a useful lever for re-engagement or deadline-driven sends. With only one urgency-framed email in the dataset, this is directional only.

    Vague curiosity-only subject lines ('Your insights are needed!') underperform compared to incentive or urgency framing.

    26.0% open rate vs. 33.1% for incentive+personalization — a 7.1pp gap.
    Show reasoning

    'Your insights are needed!' relies solely on a generic appeal without a specific hook, incentive, or urgency cue, and it lands in third place by open rate despite having the second-largest send volume. This suggests value-signal or urgency is more effective than vague appeals, though list size differences are a confound.

    Click rate does not consistently follow open rate — the largest list had a higher click rate (4.4%) than the 'Last call' email (4.2%) despite lower opens.

    6.5% click rate for 'Your insights are needed!' — highest click rate despite only the third-best open rate.
    Show reasoning

    'Your insights are needed!' achieved the highest click rate (6.5%) while ranking third on opens, suggesting its body content or CTA converted curious openers effectively. This disconnect between open and click rank is worth noting but the dataset is too small to generalize.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear performance pattern in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~25 to ~60 characters with no monotonic relationship to open rate.

    The top-performing email has a mid-length subject (~38 chars), the lowest performer is longer (~40 chars), and middle performers vary widely. No consistent length trend is visible across the five emails.

    Send timing (day/time) cannot be evaluated from this dataset.

    Dates span 2025-09-17 to 2026-06-29 with no time-of-day data available.

    Only calendar dates are available, not send times or days of week in a meaningful comparable cluster. The lone 2026 send (n=10) is too small to draw any conclusions, and the 2025 sends are spread across September–October without enough variation to identify a timing pattern.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 5 emails, which is too small to draw statistically reliable conclusions from any single pattern. List sizes vary dramatically — from 10 to 1,179 recipients — meaning open and click rates are not directly comparable across sends; smaller lists are more susceptible to noise. The 10-recipient send ('Share your experience with Medallion?') is essentially an outlier and should be excluded from strategic interpretation. No subject lines are repeated verbatim across sends, so there is no A/B test structure to exploit. The unresolved Handlebars token ('{{ contact.firstname }}') in one subject line also raises a data quality flag: if this token failed to render for some recipients, the true open rate impact of personalization may be overstated or understated.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails by open rate share a 'now available' or 'is now live' framing that announces something concrete and already delivered, rather than teasing something upcoming. Specificity in subject lines — naming the exact feature or report — appears to correlate with higher opens, while vague or forward-looking language underperforms. Notably, clicks are nearly absent across all emails, suggesting that even engaged openers are not converting to action, which is the primary gap to close next time.

    Strong Signal

    Click-through rates are effectively zero across the entire dataset, indicating a systemic CTA or content problem.

    4 out of 5 emails have a 0.0% click rate; only one email achieved a 9.1% click rate

    Try this next: Audit the email body and CTA for all product release emails — ensure there is a single, prominent, action-oriented link (e.g., 'Try it now' or 'See the new reports') and test whether adding a direct deep-link to the feature improves click rates.

    Moderate Signal

    'Now live' or 'now available' subject line framing outperforms future-tense or teaser language.

    51.7% average open rate for 'now available/live' subjects vs. 38.2% for forward-looking subjects
    Show reasoning

    The three highest-opening emails use present-tense delivery language ('is now available', 'is now live'), while the two lowest use anticipatory framing ('is coming', 'will go live soon'). The pattern is directional but the sample is only 5 emails and audience sizes differ, so causality is uncertain.

    Specific, feature-named subject lines tend to open higher than generic experience-focused ones.

    60.0% open rate for the most specific subject line vs. 26.3% for the least specific
    Show reasoning

    The highest-opening email names the exact reports ('Provider Summary and Credentialing'), while 'A better analytics experience is coming your way' is more abstract. However, the send sizes are small and differ, making it hard to isolate subject line specificity as the sole driver.

    The one email that generated clicks used a profile-completion feature topic, which may be more personally actionable for recipients.

    9.1% click rate for 'New profile completion logic is now live' vs. 0.0% for all analytics-focused emails
    Show reasoning

    Analytics report emails generated zero clicks even at high open rates, while the profile-completion email — which directly affects the user's own record — was the only one to drive clicks. This suggests that self-relevant, task-oriented features may prompt more action than reporting or analytics features, but with only one data point per category this remains directional.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~35 to ~70 characters with no monotonic relationship to open rate

    The highest-opening email has a long subject line (~65 chars) and the lowest-opening has a medium-length one (~50 chars), so length alone does not explain performance differences here. No actionable length pattern is supported.

    Send timing (day of week or time of month) does not show a discernible pattern given the data available.

    Dates span July–September with no clustering of high or low performers by time period

    Emails were sent on various dates in July, August, and September, and open rates do not correlate with send month or apparent send cadence. With only 5 data points and no send-time metadata, no timing conclusion can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 5 emails with send sizes ranging from 10 to 20 recipients — this is an extremely small sample and no finding should be treated as statistically significant. The subject line 'New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing' appears twice (sent 2025-09-10 and 2025-09-17), likely to the same or overlapping segments on consecutive weeks; this duplication inflates representation of that message and the second send's lower open rate (40% vs. 60%) may partly reflect recipient fatigue or overlap rather than a true independent observation. Audience sizes are not uniform, which means raw open rates are not directly comparable without weighting.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 335 72.5% 3.9%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  246 63.0% 7.7%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 236 55.1% 4.2%
    Watch expertise behind every outcome: Medallion Demo Day 97 54.6% 4.1%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 238 52.5% 2.9%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing webinar emails in this dataset consistently use urgency, personalization, and curiosity-driven subject lines tied to specific events or tangible incentives (gift boxes, coffee kits, cocktails). Emails with smaller, more targeted audience sizes tend to show higher open rates, but even large sends (1,500+) achieved 40%+ open rates when tied to the Elevate conference brand or named speakers. The clearest opportunity is to lean into scarcity language ('Last chance,' 'You're so close') and concrete incentives, while retiring generic 'watch on demand' follow-ups which consistently underperform. Click rates suggest that urgency alone drives opens but content relevance drives clicks — emails with specific value propositions (credentialing timelines, cost reduction) convert better than event-brand emails.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and scarcity language in subject lines drives above-average open rates across large sends.

    43.8% open rate on '⏰ Last Chance! Time is running out' sent to 1,542 recipients

    Try this next: Include a time-bound urgency phrase in at least one email per webinar sequence, ideally in the final send before the event.

    'Watch on demand' and post-event follow-up subject lines are the worst performers in this dataset.

    0.0% open rate across three separate 'watch on demand' or post-event sends

    Try this next: Replace passive 'available on demand' subject lines with missed-opportunity framing such as 'We missed you' or 'Catch what you missed' to maintain engagement after the live event.

    Credentialing and payer enrollment topic emails generate strong click-through rates relative to their open rates.

    7.7% click rate on 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' (n=246) and 9.0% on '[You're invited] Executive perspectives on cost, risk, and innovation' (n=288)

    Try this next: Prioritize credentialing and payer enrollment webinar content for audiences where conversion (not just awareness) is the goal, and test these subject line themes on larger lists.

    The Elevate conference brand sustains high open rates (38–72%) across the full campaign arc from initial invite to last-chance sends.

    40.7% open rate on first large Elevate send (n=1,748) rising to 43.8% on last-chance send (n=1,542)

    Try this next: Maintain a 4–6 email sequence for major branded events, with the final 1–2 sends using explicit urgency framing, as this appears to be where open rate peaks occur.

    Moderate Signal

    Tangible physical incentives (coffee kits, gift boxes, cocktails) lift open rates but do not reliably lift click rates.

    55.6% open rate and 33.3% click rate on 'You're So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today' (n=9)
    Show reasoning

    Gift box and coffee kit subject lines achieve high open rates (44–56%), but the sample sizes are very small (9–24 recipients), making these results unreliable. The cocktail-themed emails sent to larger audiences ('This demo ends with a drink,' n=379) performed poorly at 21.6%, suggesting novelty wears off at scale or that the audience segment matters significantly.

    Personalization tokens in subject lines do not show a measurable lift over non-personalized urgency lines.

    41.3% open rate for personalized 'Elevate is only 2 weeks away' (n=1,652) vs. 43.8% for non-personalized 'Last Chance! Time is running out' (n=1,542)
    Show reasoning

    The two personalized token emails ('{{ personalization_token }}') sent to 1,652 and 1,570 recipients achieved 41.3% and 38.9% open rates respectively — comparable to but not clearly better than non-personalized urgency lines at similar audience sizes. The personalization token itself also appears broken/unfired in the subject line, which may have suppressed performance.

    State-specific personalization (fastest/slowest enrollment timeline states) achieves solid open rates but low click rates.

    50.2% open rate for 'fastest enrollment timeline states' (n=217) vs. 2.3% click rate
    Show reasoning

    Both state-segmented emails achieved ~49–50% open rates, which is strong for their audience size. However, click rates of 2.0–2.3% are among the lower rates for credentialing-topic emails, suggesting the segmentation drives curiosity but the email body or CTA may not deliver enough specific value to motivate action.

    No Signal

    Shorter, curiosity-gap subject lines (under 60 characters) do not consistently outperform longer, descriptive lines.

    Open rates range from 21.6% to 55.1% for both short and long subject lines

    The data does not support a clean subject-length effect. 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' (55 chars, 55.1% open rate) outperforms shorter lines like 'This demo ends with a drink' (27 chars, 21.6%). Length alone is not a reliable predictor of performance in this dataset.

    Send timing and day-of-week patterns cannot be reliably assessed from this dataset.

    Emails span 2025-07-08 through 2026-06-30 with no day-of-week metadata available

    The dataset includes dates but not send times or days of the week in a parseable format, and the emails represent different campaigns, audience sizes, and event phases — making it impossible to isolate timing as a variable from this data alone.

    Data quality notes: Several emails were sent to audiences of 1–10 recipients, making their open and click rates statistically meaningless (e.g., three emails with n=1 show 100% open rates). At least three subject lines appear in multiple rows with different send dates and audience sizes, likely representing A/B test variants or re-sends to unsegmented lists — these should not be treated as independent data points. The broken personalization token appearing literally in two subject lines ('{{ personalization_token(…) }}') indicates a technical error that likely suppressed performance for those sends. All analysis here treats audience size as a significant confounder and weights patterns seen in sends of 100+ recipients more heavily.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 218 49.5% 3.2%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 342 44.1% 5.9%
     Why hiring isn’t improving specialty access 117 43.6% 2.6%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 356 41.6% 4.2%
     Scaling 700+ clinics without slowing down 120 40.8% 2.5%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing emails in this dataset combine urgent, problem-framing subject lines with specific financial or operational stakes (e.g., '$1M+', '30 days to 6'), and benefit from being sent to smaller, likely more targeted segments. The highest open rates cluster around care access and provider readiness themes introduced in early-to-mid 2026, while the hospital-closure and operational-resilience series from mid-2025 performed more modestly. Critically, high open rates are almost entirely decoupled from clicks — the rare emails that drove both opens and clicks tended to be report-anchored sends to larger lists. To improve, pair strong problem-framing subject lines with a clear, compelling click destination rather than relying on opens alone as a success metric.

    Strong Signal

    Problem-framing subject lines focused on care access and provider readiness generate the highest open rates.

    7 of the top 10 open rates belong to emails referencing 'access,' 'provider readiness,' or 'enrollment' bottlenecks

    Try this next: Lead future campaigns with subject lines that name a specific, audience-felt problem in the care access or credentialing space, especially when you can contrast a flawed common assumption (e.g., 'hiring won't fix it').

    Report-anchored emails are the only content type that consistently drives meaningful click rates.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' averaged 2.4% click rate across three sends, the highest sustained click performance in the dataset

    Try this next: Anchor every content email to a specific downloadable asset, data report, or interactive tool, and make the click destination explicit in the subject line (e.g., '[Report]', '[Data]').

    Smaller send lists (under 50 recipients) dominate the top open rates, making it impossible to separate subject line quality from list targeting quality.

    All 5 emails with open rates above 50% were sent to 36 or fewer recipients

    Try this next: When testing subject line concepts, ensure test segments are large enough (100+ recipients) to distinguish list quality from message resonance before scaling.

    Moderate Signal

    Quantified stakes in subject lines correlate with strong open rates on larger lists.

    '[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)' achieved 58.3% opens on 36 recipients
    Show reasoning

    The $1M+ specificity stands out as the clearest financial hook in the dataset, but the send volume is only 36, making it hard to isolate the dollar figure as the driver versus list quality. 'From 30 days to 6' (50%) also uses a quantified improvement, though sent to only 2 recipients.

    Bracket-formatted subject lines with event or asset labels ([Report], [You're invited], [Final chance]) appear in several top-10 performers but also appear in lower performers, so the signal is directional only.

    3 of the top 6 open rates include bracket formatting; 1 of the bottom 5 also uses brackets
    Show reasoning

    Bracket prefixes like '[Report]' and '[You're invited]' appear among the highest open rates, but the lowest-performing '[Report]' send (19.9%) shows the format alone doesn't guarantee performance — list segment and content relevance matter more.

    The 'coffee' themed subject lines (emoji, latte pun) achieved mixed results, with open rates but near-zero clicks.

    '☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue?' opened at 47.6% but had 0.0% click rate on 42 sends
    Show reasoning

    The coffee/latte angle ('A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays' at 24.3%, '[Final chance for coffee]' at 60%) produces decent opens but zero or near-zero clicks across the board, suggesting the playful framing attracts attention without creating enough urgency or clarity to drive action.

    No Signal

    Shorter, plain-language subject lines do not clearly outperform longer or bracket-formatted ones across this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short lines ('Why care access is breaking down') and longer bracket-formatted ones ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)')

    Subject line length varies widely among top performers with no consistent directional signal — the highest open rate is a short conversational line, but the 4th-highest is a long bracketed line. Length alone does not appear to be a reliable predictor of open rate in this data.

    No clear send-timing or send-day pattern is visible in this dataset.

    Top open rates span dates from 2025-07-24 through 2026-05-21 with no clustering by day or month

    The data includes dates but not send times, and emails are spread across nearly a year with high open rates appearing in July 2025, October 2025, January 2026, April 2026, and May 2026. No single month, season, or apparent day-of-week pattern emerges from the available data.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple sends on the same date (e.g., 'Test your operational readiness with this worksheet' sent to 3 separate segments of 19, 215, and 21 recipients; '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' sent to 218, 82, and 136 recipients), indicating list segmentation rather than true A/B tests — these should not be interpreted as independent observations. Additionally, 13 of the 31 emails were sent to fewer than 50 recipients, which inflates open rates due to small denominators and likely warmer or more targeted audiences; patterns observed on these micro-sends should be validated on larger lists before drawing conclusions.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 55 56.4% 1.8%
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 76 47.4% 6.6%
    Dinner @ Stillwell's ? 88 44.3% 3.4%
    Tomorrow: Join us for Medallion’s Las Vegas Golden Knights Experience 56 35.7% 3.6%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 280 35.7% 2.1%
    What this tells us

    The highest-opening emails in this dataset share two traits: they name a specific, prestigious venue or unique experience in the subject line, and they were sent to smaller, more targeted lists. Casual, conversational subject lines like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' also outperformed formal 'Don't Miss' or 'Last Chance' urgency framings, suggesting this audience responds better to intrigue than pressure. Conference-anchored invitations (e.g., JPM) and novelty experiences (Sphere, Golden Knights) consistently outperformed generic dinner invitations. The clearest lever to pull next time is replacing urgency/scarcity language with specific venue or experience names in the subject line.

    Strong Signal

    Naming a specific, distinctive venue or experience in the subject line drives higher open rates than generic dinner framing.

    Top 5 emails by open rate (44–56%) all name a specific venue or experience; bottom 5 average 12% open rate with generic framing.

    Try this next: Always name the specific venue or experience in the subject line rather than relying on 'exclusive dinner' as the hook.

    Urgency and scarcity subject lines ('Last Chance,' 'Don't Miss,' 'Final Call') underperform compared to invitation-forward language.

    Six urgency-framed emails averaged 19.3% open rate vs. 35%+ for invitation-framed emails in the top half.

    Try this next: Lead with the invitation and the experience name; reserve urgency language for only the final follow-up send, if at all.

    Moderate Signal

    Conversational, casual subject lines ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') match or beat formal invitation language.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' averaged 47% open rate across two sends vs. 25% average for formal 'A Seat at the Table' framing.
    Show reasoning

    The Stillwell's emails used minimal punctuation formality and a question mark, achieving 44–50% open rates. However, the sample is small (two sends, 14 and 88 recipients) and the audience targeting may differ from the formal sends, making it hard to isolate subject line style as the sole variable.

    Conference- or event-anchored subjects (naming the conference like JPM) show strong open rates but have limited examples.

    The two JPM-anchored emails achieved 47.4% and 56.4% open rates, the highest single-event pair in the dataset.
    Show reasoning

    Both sends referencing JPM week performed in the top 4 across the entire dataset. Anchoring to a known industry conference may prime recipients who are already thinking about the event. However, there are only two sends to compare against, and smaller list size may contribute.

    Smaller send lists (under 60 recipients) tend to produce higher open rates, but the correlation is imperfect.

    Median open rate for sends under 60 recipients is 35% vs. 22% for sends over 100 recipients.
    Show reasoning

    The top-opening emails were mostly sent to 8–76 recipients, while the lowest-performing emails were sent to 83–283. This could reflect tighter audience targeting rather than list size itself, and one send to 280 recipients (Aquavit) still achieved 35.7%, complicating a clean conclusion.

    Higher click rates loosely correlate with unique or experiential events rather than dinner-only invitations.

    The Wizard of Oz Sphere email had a 6.2% click rate; the JPM dinner had a 6.6% click rate — both among the highest click rates in the set.
    Show reasoning

    Experiential or conference-tied events (Sphere, Golden Knights, JPM dinner, Boston healthcare dinner) show click rates of 5–6.6%, while generic dinner invitations frequently show 0% click rates. This suggests recipients who open experience-specific emails are also more motivated to act. However, click rates overall are low across the board, limiting confidence.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Top open rate (56.4%) belongs to a 56-character subject; second place (50%) is tied between a 22-character and a 79-character subject.

    There is no clear length-based pattern: the top performers span from very short ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?', ~22 chars) to medium-long ('Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM', ~56 chars). Length alone does not appear to be a meaningful predictor of open rate in this dataset.

    Send timing (day of week or time of year) does not show a clear pattern in this data.

    High-performing emails span January, February, April, October, and December — no seasonal cluster.

    Top-performing emails are distributed across multiple months and years with no obvious seasonal concentration. Day-of-week data is not available in this dataset, preventing any send-timing analysis below the monthly level.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is 31 emails, but several subject lines were sent multiple times to different or overlapping segments (e.g., 'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' sent twice; 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent three times; 'In Vegas? Join us for Medallion's Emerald Evening' sent twice; 'A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit' sent twice), making it difficult to isolate subject line effects from audience targeting effects. List sizes vary dramatically (1 to 283 recipients), and smaller lists may represent more curated, warm audiences, inflating their open rates relative to larger cold sends. One email was sent to only a single recipient (0% open rate) and should be excluded from any aggregate analysis. Confidence in any individual pattern is limited given the small total number of unique campaigns.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
     $43M raised to help healthcare organizations scale faster 246 44.7% 6.1%
    $43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse 147 20.4% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset consistently use specific, action-oriented language that signals immediate relevance — phrases like 'Now live,' 'Action required,' and named feature announcements drove open rates above 60% even at meaningful send volumes. Emails framing urgency around compliance changes or new capabilities outperformed broader company news like the funding announcement, which underperformed significantly at scale. Click rates remain near zero for most emails, suggesting recipients open to stay informed but rarely need to click, with the exception of fee changes and required-field updates that prompted action. The clearest takeaway is to lead subjects with what changed and why it matters to the recipient's workflow, rather than leading with company milestones.

    Strong Signal

    Subjects signaling immediate platform changes ('Now live,' 'Action required') drive the highest open rates.

    67.7%–71.4% open rates for 'Now live' and 'Action required' subjects vs. 20–25% for funding announcement subjects

    Try this next: Lead every announcement subject with a status marker ('Now live:' or 'Action required:') to immediately signal operational relevance rather than company news.

    Compliance and regulatory-change subjects generate above-average opens and the highest click rates.

    8.6% click rate for 'New feature: Track closed practice locations' and 7.1% for 'Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon'

    Try this next: When announcing compliance-driven changes, pair the subject with a clear CTA in the email body; this audience will click when action is genuinely required.

    Company funding/milestone announcements significantly underperform feature-specific announcements.

    The three '$43M raised' variants averaged ~30% open rate vs. ~57% average for feature announcements

    Moderate Signal

    Sending to very small lists (n=1 to n=7) produces artificially high open rates that are not meaningful benchmarks.

    The two n=1 sends show 100% open rates; the n=7 send shows 71.4%
    Show reasoning

    Several top-ranked emails were sent to audiences of 1–7 recipients, where a single open or non-open swings the rate by 14–100 percentage points. These dominate the top of the open-rate ranking but cannot be used to draw conclusions about subject line effectiveness at scale. Excluding sub-10 sends, the effective top performers start at the 'Now live' email (n=31, 67.7%).

    'Coming soon' preview emails show no lift in the subsequent 'Now live' email's open rate, suggesting teaser sequences don't compound engagement.

    'Coming soon: Provider profile redesign' (n=48, 47.9%) preceded 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' (n=31, 67.7%) — different list sizes make direct comparison difficult
    Show reasoning

    Three 'Coming soon' / 'Now live' pairs exist in the data. In each case the 'Now live' version matches or outperforms the preview, but the audience sizes differ enough that we cannot isolate a pure sequencing effect. The data does not show that 'Coming soon' priming meaningfully depresses or boosts the follow-up open rate.

    Emails announcing new analytics reports show consistent mid-range performance (~44–80% opens) but near-zero clicks, suggesting informational value without driving platform visits.

    6 analytics announcement emails averaged ~55% open rate with 0% click rate (except one at 3.7%)
    Show reasoning

    Analytics report announcements reliably achieve above-median open rates, possibly because they signal new value in the product. However, only one of six generated any clicks, implying recipients read the subject, feel informed, and don't navigate to the platform. This is a missed engagement opportunity for a feature designed to drive platform usage.

    No Signal

    Subjects in the 50–70 character range appear in both top and bottom performers, suggesting subject length alone does not predict performance.

    Top performer 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here\'s what to do' is ~65 chars; bottom performer '$43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse' is ~67 chars

    After reviewing subject lengths across the dataset, there is no consistent correlation between character count and open rate. Short and long subjects appear throughout all performance tiers. The content and framing of the subject — not its length — appears to be the operative variable in this dataset.

    No send-timing pattern is detectable from this data.

    Top open rates span dates from October 2025 through April 2026 with no clustering by day or month

    The dataset includes dates but not send times, and emails are spread across multiple months without enough volume on any single day to identify a day-of-week effect. High performers appear in January, February, and April; low performers appear in August and October. Seasonal or day-of-week conclusions cannot be drawn from 28 emails lacking time-of-day metadata.

    Data quality notes: Several significant caveats apply: (1) Six emails were sent to audiences of 1–7 recipients, making their open rates statistically meaningless — the top two entries at 100% were sent to a single recipient each. (2) The same subject line 'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' appears twice (n=5 and n=24) and 'New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing' also appears twice (n=12 and n=37), likely representing segmented sends of the same campaign — these should be aggregated for true performance measurement. (3) Audience sizes range from 1 to 246, so raw open-rate comparisons across emails conflate list-composition effects with subject-line effects. (4) Click rates are 0% for the majority of emails, limiting conclusions about CTAs to a small subset of sends. Insights are most reliable for sends with n≥20.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Medallion Product Update - February 2026 76 61.8% 2.6%
    Medallion Product Update - April 2026 73 58.9% 0.0%
    Your August Roundup ✨ 53 58.5% 1.9%
    Medallion Product Update - May 2026 76 56.6% 0.0%
    Your October Roundup ✨ 52 53.8% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Newsletter emails in this dataset consistently achieve strong open rates (42–72% across meaningful send volumes), suggesting a highly engaged subscriber base rather than dramatic differences driven by subject line choices alone. The 'Roundup' emoji-branded series and the rebranded 'Product Update' series perform comparably in open rates, but neither format reliably drives clicks — zero click rates are common across both. The most actionable gap is click engagement: with most emails generating 0% clicks despite strong opens, the content or CTAs inside the emails are failing to convert curious readers into active users. Prioritizing a single, clear in-email CTA that gives readers something to do would be the highest-leverage next step.

    Strong Signal

    Click rates are consistently near zero regardless of subject line or format, pointing to an in-email content problem.

    7 of 11 emails have a 0.0% click rate; maximum observed is 3.5%

    Try this next: Add one prominent, specific CTA button per email linked to a product feature, case study, or action relevant to that month's update, and A/B test its placement above the fold.

    Moderate Signal

    The 'Roundup' branded series generates higher open rates than the 'Product Update' series, but only at smaller list sizes.

    Roundup avg open rate 63.2% (avg 37 recipients) vs. Product Update avg 57.3% (avg 75 recipients)
    Show reasoning

    The Roundup emails were sent to smaller, likely earlier-stage lists and achieved higher average opens, while Product Update emails went to ~2x more recipients with somewhat lower opens.
