This could reflect list dilution as the audience grew rather than a subject line branding effect. The pattern is directional but confounded by list size growth over time.

    List size growth over time correlates with declining open rates, suggesting list quality dilution.

    Open rate dropped from ~65% average (1–35 recipients, mid-2025) to ~57% average (73–76 recipients, early-2026)
    Show reasoning

    As the subscriber list grew from single digits to 76 recipients, average open rates trended downward. This is a common list-growth pattern where newer subscribers are less engaged. The dataset is too small to be definitive, but the direction is consistent.

    No Signal

    Emoji in subject lines (✨) does not clearly outperform plain subject lines on open rate.

    58.5% avg open rate for emoji subjects vs. 57.3% for non-emoji subjects

    The 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' emails and the plain 'Medallion Product Update - [Month]' emails overlap heavily in open rates (42–72% vs. 52–62%). Any difference is explained by audience size and list maturity, not the presence of an emoji. No directional pattern is visible.

    Subject line length does not show a consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~22 to ~38 characters with no monotonic open-rate trend

    Shorter subjects like 'Your June Roundup ✨' (22 chars) hit 100% but on a single send, while mid-length 'Medallion Product Update - February 2026' (40 chars) achieves 61.8% on 76 recipients. No clear length-based pattern holds across the dataset.

    Send timing (day of month, season) does not show a reliable pattern in this dataset.

    Open rates range from 42.3% to 72.4% with sends spread across all months

    Emails sent in early, mid, and late month, and across all seasons, show overlapping open rates. September's roundup (sent October) is the lowest at 42.3%, but this could reflect list size or content rather than timing. No send-day or send-time data is available to analyze intra-week timing.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 11 emails with send volumes ranging from 1 to 76 recipients — extremely small audiences that make open and click rates highly volatile (e.g., the 100% open rate on 'Your June Roundup ✨' is a single-recipient send and should be excluded from trend analysis). No subject lines are repeated across sends. The growing list size over time confounds any subject line or format comparisons, since earlier sends went to a smaller, likely more self-selected audience. Click rate conclusions are limited because even one click on a 30-person list equals ~3.3%, so 'zero clicks' may mean literally 0 or 1 person clicked.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 619 38.8% 4.5%
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 720 37.5% 5.7%
    Your insights are needed! 737 35.5% 5.4%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 748 20.9% 5.2%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails share urgency framing and/or clear value exchange (a $20 incentive), with the highest open rate going to a 'last call' urgency subject line and strong performers also using direct incentive callouts. The weakest opener led with the incentive in a formulaic 'chance to' phrasing, suggesting that word choice and framing matter more than simply including a reward. Personalization via first name token also appeared in a high-click email but rendered as a broken merge tag, indicating a technical issue that likely hurt performance. For future sends, prioritize urgency language or direct incentive framing while fixing personalization tokens before deployment.

    Strong Signal

    Emails sent to very small audiences are not comparable to bulk sends and skew performance metrics.

    13 recipients for the June 2026 send vs. 619–748 for all others

    Try this next: Exclude sends under ~100 recipients from aggregate performance comparisons to avoid misleading averages.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency framing ('last call') drove the highest open rate among survey emails.

    38.8% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey'
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing subject line used 'last call' urgency and achieved the highest open rate at 38.8%. However, with only 5 emails and no A/B test isolating urgency as the sole variable, this is directional rather than conclusive. Other factors like audience segment and send timing may also contribute.

    A broken personalization token (unrendered first name) coincided with a high-performing email, but likely suppressed its potential.

    37.5% open rate despite '{{ contact.firstname }}' rendering as a literal token
    Show reasoning

    The second-highest open rate email contained an unrendered merge tag in the subject line, meaning recipients saw '{{ contact.firstname }}' instead of their name. The email still performed well (37.5% open, 5.7% click), suggesting the $20 incentive carried it, but the broken token likely cost opens among recipients who found it off-putting.

    Direct incentive callouts in subject lines are associated with above-average open and click rates.

    5.7% and 5.4% click rates for the two emails explicitly mentioning '$20' or 'insights'
    Show reasoning

    Three of the top four emails by open rate either mention '$20' or frame the ask around the respondent's insights. The lowest-performing email ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20') also mentions $20 but underperformed significantly (20.9% open rate), suggesting incentive mention alone is insufficient — framing and word choice matter. The sample is too small to isolate the incentive effect cleanly.

    Vague, low-urgency subject lines ('Your chance to provide feedback') produce substantially lower open rates.

    20.9% open rate — the lowest in the set — for 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20'
    Show reasoning

    This email had the largest audience (748 sends) and still achieved the lowest open rate, with the same $20 incentive as higher performers. The 'your chance to' phrasing is indirect and non-urgent compared to peers. While audience fatigue or send order (it was sent first in the sequence) may also be factors, the wording pattern stands out as a likely contributor.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~30 to ~60 characters with no clear trend

    The top opener is among the longer subjects ('Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey') while shorter ones like 'Your insights are needed!' also perform well (35.5%). There is no monotonic or consistent pattern between length and open rate across these 5 emails.

    Send timing patterns cannot be reliably assessed from this dataset.

    Emails sent across 5 different dates (Sep 17 to Jun 29 the following year)

    All five emails were sent on different dates spanning roughly 9 months, with no repeated days of week or time data provided. The one outlier send (June 2026) had only 13 recipients, making any timing comparison unreliable. No send-day or send-time pattern can be extracted from this data.

    Data quality notes: This analysis covers only 5 emails, which is too small a sample to draw statistically significant conclusions — all insights should be treated as directional hypotheses. Audience sizes vary substantially: four emails were sent to 619–748 recipients, but one (June 2026) was sent to only 13 recipients, making its metrics (30.8% open, 7.7% click) unreliable and not comparable to the others. Additionally, the September 2025 emails appear to be a sequential series sent to overlapping or identical lists, meaning later sends (e.g., 'last call') may benefit from cumulative exposure or audience self-selection among non-openers, inflating apparent performance differences due to list fatigue or engagement bias rather than subject line quality alone. One subject line contains an unrendered merge tag ('{{ contact.firstname }}'), indicating a production error. No subject lines are exact duplicates across sends.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails by open rate share a specific, feature-complete subject line pattern: naming the exact feature and its availability status ('now available'). Both 68.4% and 63.6% open-rate emails used identical subjects about 'New Analytic reports,' suggesting that concrete, outcome-stating language outperforms vaguer teaser language like 'coming your way.' The one email with a non-zero click rate ('New profile completion logic is now live') had the smallest audience, making CTR conclusions unreliable, but it is the only email that drove any clicks at all. Future sends should prioritize specific feature names and 'now live' framing over anticipatory or abstract subject lines.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines naming a specific feature and using 'now available' or 'now live' language achieve the highest open rates.

    68.4% open rate for 'New Analytic reports are now available...' vs. 52.0% for the vaguer 'A better analytics experience is coming your way'
    Show reasoning

    The two highest-opening emails both used the phrase 'now available' with a named feature, while the 'coming your way' teaser underperformed them by 16+ percentage points. However, the sample is tiny (5 emails) and audience sizes differ (19 vs. 25 recipients), so this is directional rather than conclusive.

    Emails with 'now live' or 'now available' framing are the only ones that generated any clicks at all.

    16.7% click rate for 'New profile completion logic is now live'; 0.0% for all other emails
    Show reasoning

    The only email with clicks used 'now live' in the subject and presumably linked to the live feature — suggesting that announcing something already deployed may prompt readers to act, while preview or teaser emails do not. However, this is a single data point with only 6 recipients, making it a weak signal.

    Repeating the same subject line to a different segment did not meaningfully degrade open rates.

    68.4% (n=19) vs. 63.6% (n=11) for the identical 'New Analytic reports' subject sent one week apart
    Show reasoning

    The second send to a smaller audience (likely a different or remainder segment) achieved nearly as high an open rate as the first, suggesting the subject line had durable appeal and/or the audiences were distinct. A 4.8-point drop is small, but with audiences this tiny any difference could be noise.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate performance in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~36 to ~74 characters with no consistent trend vs. open rate

    The top two emails have the longest subject lines (~74 chars) and highest open rates, but the bottom three emails vary widely in length while clustering around the same 50% open rate. The tiny sample and near-identical open rates in the lower group make it impossible to isolate length as a driver.

    Send timing (day of week or time of month) shows no discernible pattern relative to open rate.

    Emails sent across July, August, and September all fall within a 50–68% open rate range

    Dates range from July 9 to September 17 with no consistent day-of-week or timing advantage visible. The small sample of 5 emails and differing audience sizes prevent any timing conclusion from being drawn.

    Emoji use in subject lines does not appear to improve open rates in this dataset.

    50.0% open rate for the subject with a ✅ emoji, matching the non-emoji email at the same rate

    The one email using an emoji ('A new approach to profile completion will go live soon ✅') achieved a 50.0% open rate, identical to the non-emoji profile-completion email. No advantage from emoji use is visible, though the sample is far too small to generalize.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 5 emails with audience sizes ranging from 6 to 25 recipients — far too small to draw statistically significant conclusions. Two emails share an identical subject line sent to different segments one week apart, which inflates apparent subject-line pattern confidence. The single email with a non-zero click rate had only 6 recipients, meaning one click produced a 16.7% rate; this is not a reliable benchmark. All findings should be treated as directional hypotheses to test with larger sends.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 105 74.3% 5.7%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  113 60.2% 8.8%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar 91 58.2% 4.4%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 104 56.7% 2.9%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 76 56.6% 2.6%
    What this tells us

    The highest-engagement emails in this dataset are dominated by urgency-driven subject lines tied to specific named events (Elevate, Demo Day) and personalized or niche-segmented sends with very small audience sizes — both of which inflate open rates artificially. Stripping out sends under ~20 recipients, the strongest legitimate performers use clear urgency cues ('Last chance,' 'Last Chance!', countdown language) combined with a concrete value proposition or curiosity hook ('bitter truth about payer enrollment delays,' 'coffee, chocolate'). The weakest performers tend to be early-funnel, broad-audience sends with generic agenda or invitation framing. For future campaigns, prioritize urgency-framed subject lines with a specific hook over general invitation language, and treat small-list sends as directionally interesting but not statistically reliable benchmarks.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and deadline language in subject lines consistently drives higher open rates among large-send emails.

    60.2% open rate for 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' (n=113) vs. 25.4% for 'Are you registered? Credentialing & enrollment automation w/a twist' (n=142)

    Try this next: Lead late-stage promotional emails with explicit deadline language ('Last chance,' 'X days left') rather than soft invitation copy — make the scarcity visible in the subject line itself.

    Broad early-funnel invitation emails sent to 600–900 recipients consistently underperform compared to event-specific or urgency emails.

    Emails sent to 594–888 recipients average 28.2% open rate; emails sent to 91–229 recipients on urgency themes average 43–60%

    Try this next: Segment broad awareness sends more tightly by role or use case, and reserve large-list sends for the highest-impact urgency moment (e.g., 48-hour countdown) rather than early-funnel invitations.

    Moderate Signal

    Curiosity-driven or sensory hook subject lines outperform straightforward topic descriptions among mid-to-large sends.

    56.6%–66.7% open rates for 'bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' and 'Coffee, chocolate, and the secrets of faster enrollments' vs. 40.7%–42.9% for their more literal counterparts sent to similar-sized lists
    Show reasoning

    'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' (n=76, 56.6%) and 'Coffee, chocolate, and the secrets of faster enrollments' (n=3, 66.7%) both outperform more literal subject lines on adjacent topics. 'Coffee, chocolate, and faster provider onboarding?' (n=98, 42.9%) is a close variant but drops 14 points, possibly because removing the concrete payoff word 'secrets' reduces the hook. The small n=3 send limits confidence, but the n=76 send provides reasonable support.

    Emails promoting on-demand or post-event watch opportunities perform surprisingly well, suggesting strong re-engagement value.

    'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand' achieved 55.6% open rate (n=9); 'Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere' achieved 62.5% (n=8)
    Show reasoning

    Several post-event or on-demand access emails rank in the upper third of the dataset. While most of these have very small send lists (n=8–35) which limits confidence, the pattern is consistent across Elevate and Demo Day series. 'Watch expertise behind every outcome: Medallion Demo Day' (n=35, 54.3%) is the largest-list example and still performs well, suggesting the on-demand angle has real appeal beyond just small-list inflation.

    No Signal

    Personalization tokens in subject lines do not produce a notable open rate advantage in this dataset.

    32.4% and 30.4% open rates for the two personalization-token subject lines (n=648 and n=619)

    Both emails using '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' (which appears to have rendered as a broken fallback for many recipients) achieved open rates of 30-32%, below the dataset median for comparably sized sends. There is no evidence here that first-name personalization lifted performance, and the broken token rendering may have actively suppressed it. No clean personalized-vs-not A/B comparison exists in the data.

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate performance in this dataset.

    Subjects ranging from ~4 words to ~18 words appear throughout both the top and bottom of the open-rate rankings

    Short subjects like 'AI: Empathetic or Efficient?' (24.5%, n=469) and long ones like '[Final chance to join]: Navigating cost, innovation and credentialing and payer enrollment trends' (36.7%, n=229) both perform modestly. High performers include both short ('Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit,' 50%) and long ('Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day,' 60.2%) lines. Length alone does not predict performance in this sample.

    No meaningful send-timing pattern (day or period) is discernible from this dataset.

    Top open rates appear across May 2025 through June 2026 with no clustering by month or apparent day-of-week signal

    The data provides send dates but not send times, and the emails span over a year of intermittent campaign activity tied to specific events rather than a regular cadence. High and low performers are distributed across the calendar without a detectable seasonal or day-of-week pattern. Without day-of-week data and with event-driven scheduling dominating, no timing conclusion is warranted.

    Data quality notes: Thirteen emails were sent to 10 or fewer recipients, making their open and click rates (often 50–100%) statistically meaningless and excluded from strong-confidence conclusions. The dataset also contains at least two subject lines sent multiple times to different segments (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' appears at both n=76 and n=108; '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation...' appears at n=261 and n=5), which could reflect A/B tests or re-sends to non-openers — these should not be treated as independent data points. One subject line contains an unrendered personalization token, indicating a deliverability or template error. Audience sizes range from 1 to 888, creating extreme variance in statistical reliability across the 57 emails.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    $43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse 88 12.5% 1.1%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing announcement emails in this dataset are overwhelmingly sent to very small audiences (1–10 recipients), which makes open rate comparisons across the full list unreliable. Setting aside the micro-sends, the clearest actionable patterns are: urgency and specificity in subject lines (action-required framing and named features) outperform vague or benefit-led fundraising announcements, and 'coming soon' preview emails tend to outperform their corresponding 'now live' counterparts. The one email with a meaningful click rate was the $43M fundraising announcement sent to 31 recipients, suggesting milestone news can drive clicks when the audience is warm and targeted. For future sends, lean into specific feature names, pair preview emails with live-launch follow-ups, and avoid splitting the same announcement across many small segments which muddies performance signals.

    Strong Signal

    The $43M fundraising announcement performed very differently depending on audience size — smaller, warmer lists opened and clicked far more.

    48.4% open + 3.2% CTR at n=31 vs. 12.5% open + 1.1% CTR at n=88 for near-identical subjects on the same date

    Try this next: Segment announcement sends so that the warmest, most engaged contacts receive the email first and separately from the broad cold list.

    Click rates are effectively zero across all announcement emails except the fundraising announcement to a warm list.

    27 of 29 emails have a 0.0% click rate; only 2 emails register any clicks

    Try this next: Add a single, low-friction CTA (e.g., 'See what this means for your account') to announcement emails that introduce changes requiring user action, to test whether clicks can be activated.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and action-required framing in subject lines correlates with higher open rates.

    100% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do' (n=3)
    Show reasoning

    The 'Action required' subject line was the only mid-size send (n=3) achieving 100% open rate, and it combines urgency, specificity, and a clear consequence. However, most high open-rate emails were sent to 1 recipient, so the sample of comparable sends is too small to be definitive.

    'Coming soon' preview emails tend to outperform their corresponding 'now live' launch emails.

    'Coming soon: Standard payer names...' opened at 75% vs. 'Now live: Standard payer names...' at 62.5% (same n=8 audience)
    Show reasoning

    For the standard payer names pair, the preview email outperformed the live announcement by 12.5 percentage points with identical send sizes. The provider profile pair shows a similar direction ('Coming soon' at 70% n=10 vs. 'Now live' at 57.1% n=7), though the send sizes differ slightly. This is a consistent directional pattern across two matched pairs.

    Subject lines that name a specific feature or report type outperform generic benefit or milestone language.

    Feature-specific subjects average ~75%+ open rates (small sends) vs. fundraising milestone subjects averaging 23–48% across larger sends
    Show reasoning

    Subjects like 'Generate API keys directly in Medallion,' 'New Analytic reports are now available for New Payer Enrollment TATs,' and 'Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility' all achieved 80%+ open rates. The fundraising '$43M raised' emails, which lead with a company milestone rather than a user benefit, performed worst among the larger sends. Caveat: the feature emails went to far smaller audiences, which inflates open rates.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate in this dataset.

    Top and bottom performers span a wide range of subject line lengths with no consistent trend

    The highest open rate emails include both short subjects ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request') and long ones ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do'). The lowest performers also vary in length. No length-based pattern is detectable, likely because audience size differences dominate all other variables.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not show a reliable pattern in this dataset.

    High open rates appear across dates from August 2025 through July 2026 with no clustering

    Top-performing emails are distributed across multiple months and days with no visible clustering around specific days of the week or times of year. The dataset lacks send-time data and the audience-size confound prevents any timing conclusion.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is very limited: 29 emails total, and 17 of them were sent to 10 or fewer recipients — meaning a single open swings the rate by 10–100 percentage points and makes those figures statistically meaningless for benchmarking. The three '$43M raised' fundraising emails appear to be segmented sends of the same announcement on the same date (2025-08-18), and two 'New Analytic reports for Task Summary and Licensing' emails were sent a week apart to different audiences (n=7 and n=2), complicating direct comparison. Several subjects contain encoding artifacts (\xa0 non-breaking spaces) suggesting data extraction issues. Any insight drawn from opens-only metrics should be treated as directional at best given the small audience sizes.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 109 50.5% 2.8%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 97 46.4% 3.1%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 95 45.3% 4.2%
    Why specialty care access is breaking down 56 28.6% 1.8%
     Why hiring isn’t improving specialty access 55 27.3% 7.3%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing emails in this dataset tend to lead with concrete, specific value propositions — particularly around financial impact, enrollment bottlenecks, and state-level data — or use report/asset framing that signals immediate utility. The highest click rates cluster on emails that combine a reasonable open rate with a clear content offer (a report, a curated reading list, or a case study), suggesting that specificity drives both opens and clicks. Emails sent to very small segments (under 10 recipients) show volatile rates that are statistically unreliable. To improve future sends, prioritize subject lines that name a specific pain point or quantify the cost of inaction, and pair them with a single clear content asset rather than a broad theme.

    Strong Signal

    Subject lines referencing specific data, reports, or quantified costs outperform vague operational themes on open rate.

    50.5% open rate for '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' (n=109) vs. 27.1%–31.2% for generic 'hospital closures' and 'operational strain' subjects

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with the specific asset type (e.g., '[Report]') or a named data point rather than a conceptual theme. Test including a cost figure or geographic specificity directly in the subject line.

    Emails with a clear, single content asset (report, worksheet, curated reading list) generate the highest click rates, while thematic or narrative emails generate near-zero clicks.

    'AI in Credentialing: what to read next' hit 11.1% CTR; 'Offsetting operational and financial strain starts behind the scenes' hit 9.5% CTR — while 10 of 29 emails had 0.0% CTR

    Try this next: Attach every content email to a single named asset (report, checklist, reading list, case study). Make the asset title and format explicit in both subject and body rather than teasing a theme.

    Sending the same subject line to multiple segments produces wide performance variance, suggesting segment quality matters more than content for a given email.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' ranged from 19.8% (n=116) to 50.5% (n=109) open rate across three same-date sends

    Try this next: Audit the largest send segments for engagement history and recency. Prioritize sending to smaller, higher-engagement segments first, then test broader lists only after confirming content-market fit.

    Moderate Signal

    Bracketed label prefixes like '[Report]' and '[Final chance]' are associated with higher open rates, but the sample is small.

    Three of the top five open rates (by sends over 40) belong to subjects using bracket-formatted prefixes
    Show reasoning

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment...' and '[Final chance for coffee]' both performed above average, and '[You're invited]' hit 37.5% — though that send was only 8 recipients. The pattern is directional but confounded by content quality and segment size, making it impossible to isolate bracket formatting as the sole driver.

    Coffee-themed and hospitality-framed subject lines (used in a webinar/meeting invitation sequence) show strong opens but zero clicks, indicating engagement without conversion.

    '☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue?' — 50.0% open rate, 0.0% CTR (n=8); 'A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays' — 20.0% open rate, 1.8% CTR (n=55)
    Show reasoning

    The coffee-themed emails open well on small lists but drive no clicks, suggesting the playful framing draws curiosity without motivating action. This could reflect the invite-based nature of the content (a meeting, not an asset) or a mismatch between tone and audience intent. Sample sizes are small (4–55), limiting confidence.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines (under ~55 characters) do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Top open rates appear on both short ('AI in Credentialing: what to read next', ~38 chars) and long ('[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing', ~68 chars) subjects

    There is no discernible length threshold that separates high from low performers. The highest-volume, high-performing email has one of the longer subject lines, while some short subjects ('Hiring more providers isn't fixing access') performed at the bottom. Subject length does not appear to be a reliable predictor in this data.

    There is no detectable send-timing pattern that predicts open rate performance in this dataset.

    High open rates appear across multiple months: January (50.5%), July (40.5%), October (46.4%), November (45.3%), and May (75% — but n=4)

    Top-performing emails are distributed across 2025 and 2026 with no concentration on a particular month, quarter, or apparent day pattern. Without send-time (hour) data and with confounding variables like segment and content type, no timing conclusion is supportable.

    Data quality notes: Several important caveats apply. First, 8 of 29 sends went to fewer than 10 recipients — including the top two open rates (75% on n=4 and 60% on n=5) — making those figures statistically meaningless; insights in this report weight sends of 40+ recipients more heavily. Second, at least five subject lines appear across multiple sends on the same date, indicating A/B segment splits or sequential nurture sends to different lists; performance differences between same-subject sends reflect segment quality rather than content variation. Third, with only 29 emails total and no data on sender reputation, send time, or segment definitions, all patterns should be treated as directional hypotheses rather than confirmed conclusions.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 84 28.6% 3.6%
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 88 28.4% 9.1%
    Join Us Next Week in Boston for an Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner 83 22.9% 2.4%
    Final Call: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner at Boston’s Acclaimed TABLE 70 22.9% 7.1%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 82 20.7% 1.2%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails share two traits: they name a specific, tangible experience (a Sphere show, a named restaurant, a sports event) and they were sent to very small audiences (2–22 recipients), suggesting highly targeted lists rather than broad blasts. Casual, conversational subject lines like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' outperformed formal 'invitation'-style copy. The lowest performers were almost all Nashville/Tennessee-focused July emails sent to larger lists with vague 'exclusive evening with executives' framing and zero click-through, indicating that generic social-proof language without a concrete hook fails entirely. The playbook implication is clear: specificity of venue and experience, combined with tight list targeting, drives opens — and the click rate gap suggests RSVP friction remains a separate problem to solve.

    Strong Signal

    Naming the specific venue or experience in the subject line is strongly associated with higher open rates.

    Top 6 emails by open rate (47–62%) all name a specific venue or event; bottom 6 emails (0–11%) use generic 'exclusive evening with executives' framing.

    Try this next: Always name the specific restaurant, venue, or experience in the subject line rather than leading with 'exclusive' or audience descriptor language.

    Urgency and scarcity language ('Last Chance,' 'Final Seats,' 'Don't Miss') does not reliably lift open rates.

    Emails using urgency framing average ~20% open rate; emails without urgency framing average ~35% open rate across this dataset.

    Try this next: Deprioritize urgency/scarcity subject line formulas; if used, always pair with a specific venue or experience name rather than letting urgency carry the subject alone.

    Click rates remain low even among high-open emails, suggesting the RSVP or CTA mechanism is a separate conversion bottleneck.

    The highest click rate across all 29 emails is 25%, achieved only twice; 10 of 29 emails have a 0% click rate including several with respectable open rates.

    Try this next: Audit the in-email CTA and RSVP landing page for the high-open, zero-click emails; the subject line is working but something in the body or registration flow is losing respondents.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, punctuation-light subject lines like questions outperform formal invitation-style copy.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' averaged 46% open rate across two sends; 'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' achieved only 12.5%.
    Show reasoning

    The question-mark dinner subjects and the short informal phrasing correlate with higher opens, but sample sizes are tiny (4 and 22 sent) and the Flour + Water email had only 16 recipients, making it hard to isolate subject line style from list quality or timing. The directional signal is consistent but not conclusive.

    Emails sent to very small lists (under 25 recipients) consistently achieve higher open rates than those sent to larger lists.

    Emails sent to ≤22 recipients average ~51% open rate; emails sent to ≥27 recipients average ~22% open rate.
    Show reasoning

    The top 5 open rates all come from sends of 2–22 recipients, while the bottom half of the ranking is dominated by sends of 27–88 recipients. However, this likely reflects list quality and targeting precision rather than list size per se — smaller sends are probably to warmer, more pre-qualified prospects. Causality cannot be confirmed from open rate data alone.

    Experiential or entertainment-themed events (sports, immersive shows) generate comparable or higher open rates than fine-dining dinners.

    Las Vegas Golden Knights and Sphere experience emails range from 16.7% to 62.5% open rate, overlapping with dinner email performance of 3.2% to 54.5%.
    Show reasoning

    The Sphere/Wizard of Oz emails are the top performers overall, and Golden Knights emails are mid-range, suggesting experiential hooks are at least as compelling as restaurant names. However, the Golden Knights emails underperformed relative to the Sphere emails despite similar framing, possibly due to list quality differences or send volume.

    No Signal

    Short, conversational subject lines (under ~50 characters) do not consistently outperform longer descriptive ones in this dataset.

    The longest subject lines span a wide range: top performer 'In Vegas? Join us for Medallion's Emerald Evening: A Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience' (75 chars) sits at 62.5%; short subjects like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (22 chars) also top the list.

    Both very short and quite long subject lines appear at the top of the open rate rankings, and short subject lines also appear at the bottom (e.g., 'Don't Miss: An Exclusive Evening with Nashville Healthcare Executives'). Length alone does not explain performance in this dataset; content and specificity appear to be the confounding variable.

    There is no clear send-timing pattern (day of week or time of year) visible in this dataset.

    High open rates appear in both February 2026 (62.5%) and August 2025 (40%), and low open rates appear across July, October, and November 2025.

    Top and bottom performers are spread across months and years without a consistent seasonal or day-of-week signal. The dataset does not include send-time data, and the date field reflects send date rather than day-of-week, making timing analysis impossible to complete rigorously.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is very small overall (29 emails, most sent to under 90 recipients), making statistical significance impossible to establish for most patterns. Several subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments on different dates (e.g., 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' appears on April 23 and April 29; the Aquavit subject on September 4 and 10; the Sphere subject on February 13 and 22; the Golden Knights 'Power Play' subject on October 6 twice), which conflates subject line performance with list quality and sequence position effects. Audience size varies enormously (1 to 88 recipients), and small-list sends almost certainly reflect warmer or more pre-qualified prospects rather than a random subset of the same audience — this creates a strong confound between list quality and subject line style. No send-time data is available, only send date.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing newsletter emails consistently use the personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' format, which outperforms the more formal 'Medallion Product Update' naming convention across the board. The roundup format averages roughly 56% open rates versus ~47% for product update emails, suggesting the conversational, recipient-centric framing resonates more strongly. However, all 10 emails record a 0% click rate, indicating that while subject lines are driving opens, the content is not converting readers to action. The immediate priority should be diagnosing why no clicks are occurring — whether the emails lack clear CTAs, links, or compelling in-body offers — before optimizing further for opens.

    Strong Signal

    Click rate is 0% across all 10 emails, regardless of open rate performance.

    0.0% click rate across all 10 emails (100% of sends)

    Try this next: Audit the body content of these newsletter emails immediately to confirm that clickable CTAs or links are present, functional, and prominent; then A/B test adding a single clear CTA button in the next send.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines outperform formal 'Medallion Product Update' framing.

    ~56% avg open rate for Roundup emails vs ~47% for Product Update emails
    Show reasoning

    Five Roundup emails average approximately 56% open rate while five Product Update emails average approximately 47% open rate. The direction is consistent, but audience sizes differ significantly — Roundup emails went to 6–10 recipients while Product Update emails went to 23–27, meaning they may be reaching different, more engaged sub-segments rather than reflecting a pure subject line effect.

    Using an emoji (✨) and second-person possessive ('Your') in subject lines correlates with higher open rates.

    Top 3 open rates (75%, 66.7%, 60%) all use 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' format
    Show reasoning

    Every subject line in the top half of the open-rate ranking uses the 'Your ... ✨' pattern. However, since these emails also tend to be sent to smaller lists, audience familiarity rather than subject line wording alone may be driving the lift. The pattern is directional but cannot be fully isolated.

    Shorter subject lines (around 24–26 characters) are associated with higher open rates than longer ones.

    'Your December Roundup ✨' (26 chars, 75%) vs 'Medallion Product Update - May 2026' (35 chars, 55.6%)
    Show reasoning

    Roundup subject lines (shortest format) consistently rank higher than the longer Product Update lines. However, subject line length is confounded with the naming convention itself, so it is not possible to isolate length as an independent driver from this dataset alone.

    Smaller audience segments show higher open rates than larger ones.

    Emails sent to 6–10 recipients average ~59% open rate vs ~47% for emails sent to 23–27 recipients
    Show reasoning

    The five highest open-rate emails were all sent to 10 or fewer recipients, while the lower-performing emails went to 23–27. This could reflect a more engaged or curated sub-list, or simply statistical noise from very small absolute numbers (e.g., 75% = 6 of 8 opens). The small absolute counts make this pattern unreliable.

    No Signal

    No send-timing pattern is evident from this data.

    Emails sent across Jan, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec with no day-of-week data

    The dataset includes dates but not send times or days of the week. The monthly cadence means there is only one send per month per series, making it impossible to detect a timing pattern. No conclusion can be drawn about optimal send day or time.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is very small (10 emails total), and absolute audience sizes are extremely low — the smallest send reached only 6 recipients, meaning a single open or non-open swings the rate by 16+ percentage points. The two email series ('Your [Month] Roundup' vs 'Medallion Product Update') appear to target different sub-segments of different sizes, which means open rate differences between series likely reflect audience composition as much as subject line quality. No subject lines are repeated across sends. These factors mean all findings should be treated as directional hypotheses requiring validation on larger lists.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 291 32.0% 5.5%
    Your insights are needed! 308 28.9% 7.5%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 278 25.2% 4.7%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 220 22.3% 4.1%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails combined personalization or a monetary incentive ($20) in the subject line with concise, direct framing, achieving open rates of 25–32% across reasonably sized sends (220–308 recipients). The highest open rate went to a personalized first-name token paired with the $20 incentive, but notably this email had a lower click rate than the unpersonalized 'Your insights are needed!' email, suggesting personalization drives opens while urgency or content framing may better drive clicks. Future survey sends should test first-name personalization plus a clear incentive in the subject line, while also optimizing the email body to convert openers into clickers. The one recent send (June 2026, n=7) is too small to interpret and should be excluded from pattern analysis.

    Moderate Signal

    Including a $20 incentive in the subject line is associated with higher open rates than no incentive.

    22.3% vs 28.9% open rate for incentive vs. no-incentive subject lines (excluding the n=7 outlier)
    Show reasoning

    The two incentive-focused subject lines ('$20 for your insights!' and 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20') averaged 27.2% open rate, while the two non-incentive lines ('Your insights are needed!' and 'Last call...') averaged 27.1%—nearly identical. However, the top email combined personalization WITH the incentive, making it hard to isolate the incentive effect alone. The pattern is directional but not cleanly separable from other variables.

    First-name personalization in the subject line is associated with the highest open rate in this dataset.

    32.0% open rate for the only personalized subject line, vs. 22.3–28.9% for all non-personalized ones
    Show reasoning

    The email using '{{ contact.firstname }}' achieved the highest open rate at 32.0%, a 3.1 percentage-point lift over the next best performer. However, this email also included a $20 incentive, so the personalization effect cannot be isolated from the incentive effect. With only one personalized email in the dataset, confidence is moderate rather than strong.

    Personalization and incentives in the subject line do not guarantee the highest click rates—plain urgency-focused subject lines may outperform on clicks.

    7.5% click rate for 'Your insights are needed!' vs. 5.5% for the top open-rate email
    Show reasoning

    'Your insights are needed!' had no incentive and no personalization yet drove the highest click rate (7.5%), despite its open rate being second-highest. This suggests the email body or the subject's framing of urgency/importance may convert openers to clickers more effectively than incentive-led subjects. The sample is too small to draw firm conclusions.

    Urgency framing ('Last call') does not appear to boost performance relative to other subject lines.

    25.2% open rate for 'Last call...' — third out of four comparable sends
    Show reasoning

    'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' ranked third on open rate and second-lowest on click rate (4.7%) among the four valid sends. Urgency language alone did not differentiate this email positively. However, it may have been sent to a more fatigued segment or later in the campaign sequence, which could explain lower engagement independently of the subject line wording.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open or click rates in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~28 to ~66 characters with no monotonic trend in open rates

    The top open-rate email has a mid-length subject (~38 chars), the second-highest is shorter (~28 chars), and the third is the longest (~66 chars). There is no consistent pattern linking length to performance. With only 4 usable data points, no length-based conclusion is supported.

    Send timing (day/date) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    Top 4 emails sent across a 20-day window in Sept–Oct 2025 with no clustering by day-of-week visible

    The dates span September 17 to October 7, 2025, but day-of-week data is not provided and the sample is too small to detect timing effects. No send-timing recommendation is supported by this data.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 5 emails, four of which have usable sample sizes (220–308 recipients); the fifth (n=7, June 2026) is far too small to be statistically meaningful and was excluded from pattern analysis. All four comparable sends were made within a narrow 20-day window to what appears to be a similar audience, which limits the ability to isolate individual variable effects (e.g., personalization vs. incentive vs. send timing). No subject lines were repeated across sends. Audience sizes vary modestly (220–308), which could introduce minor noise but is unlikely to materially skew rate comparisons.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (3 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 115 78.3% 4.3%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  112 59.8% 7.1%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 103 57.3% 2.9%
    How AI is transforming hospital operations 100 54.0% 5.0%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar 81 53.1% 2.5%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing webinar emails in this dataset cluster around two themes: urgency-driven event reminders (particularly for Elevate) and personalized or targeted outreach to small segments. The highest open rates on statistically meaningful sends (100+ recipients) consistently come from deadline-framing ('Last chance,' 'Less than a month away') and curiosity-gap subject lines referencing specific pain points like payer enrollment delays or credentialing costs. Cocktail/coffee incentive hooks drove strong registrations early in the funnel but showed declining open rates over time, suggesting incentive fatigue within the same campaign. To improve, prioritize specificity over novelty—subjects naming a tangible outcome or deadline outperform clever hooks at scale.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and deadline framing in subject lines drives above-average open rates at scale.

    59.8% open rate for 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' (n=112) vs. 16.5–21.3% for non-urgent Demo Day subjects

    Try this next: Always include a specific deadline or countdown in the final 1–2 reminder emails. Use concrete language ('Wednesday,' '1 week') rather than vague urgency.

    Pain-point-specific subject lines referencing payer enrollment or credentialing outperform generic industry language.

    50.6–57.3% open rates for subjects naming 'payer enrollment delays' or 'cost and provider abrasion' vs. 27.8–35% for generic value-based care or AI framing

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with the specific problem the webinar solves (e.g., enrollment delays, credentialing risk) rather than a broad theme or industry trend label.

    Segmented or highly targeted sends (small lists) inflate apparent open rates and mask true engagement.

    7 of the top 10 open rates by percentage come from sends of 13 or fewer recipients, including four sends of 1–4 people at 100%

    Try this next: Filter performance benchmarks to sends of 50+ recipients when evaluating subject line effectiveness. Use small-list sends for relationship nurture only, not creative testing.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalization tokens (first name) in subject lines do not show a measurable open-rate advantage over non-personalized equivalents.

    36.1% and 34.1% open rates for personalized Elevate reminders vs. 37.8% for non-personalized 'Last Chance' Elevate email on comparable send volumes
    Show reasoning

    Two emails used HubSpot personalization tokens: both landed in the 34–36% range on 500+ sends, which is slightly below the non-personalized 'Last Chance' Elevate reminder (37.8%, n=508) sent close in time. However, these are different stages of the same funnel (2-week vs. 1-week vs. day-of), making direct comparison imperfect. The data does not show a clear lift from first-name tokens.

    Cocktail and coffee incentive hooks show declining open rates across sequential sends, suggesting incentive fatigue.

    Open rates fell from ~28.3% ('Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day + mixology session,' n=113) to 17.9% ('This demo ends with a drink,' n=123) and 16.5% ('Crafting better care and cocktails,' n=127) within the same July 2025 Demo Day campaign
    Show reasoning

    Three Demo Day emails leading with a drink/cocktail incentive in July 2025 show a clear decline in open rate as the campaign progressed. The coffee kit follow-up in October 2025 ('Coffee, chocolate, and faster provider onboarding?') rebounded to 43.1% (n=109), suggesting the incentive hook works better as a fresh angle than a repeated theme. Small send counts on some variants add uncertainty.

    Post-event 'missed you' and on-demand follow-up emails achieve strong open rates, indicating an underutilized nurture moment.

    'We missed you at Elevate!' hit 63.6% (n=11) and 'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand' hit 63.2% (n=19)
    Show reasoning

    Both post-event re-engagement emails performed in the top quartile of meaningful sends. This suggests non-attendees remain engaged and are receptive to on-demand content offers shortly after the event. Sample sizes are small (11–19), limiting confidence, but the pattern is directionally consistent across two separate events.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent correlation with open rate in this dataset.

    High-performing subjects range from 7 words ('How AI is transforming hospital operations,' 54%) to 18+ words ('1 Week Away: Join our upcoming info session…,' 30%)

    Both short and long subject lines appear throughout the top and bottom of the performance ranking. The best-performing large-send email by open rate ('Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day,' 59.8%) is mid-length, but so are many lower performers. No length bracket consistently outperforms others when controlling for audience size or campaign type.

    Send timing (day of week or month) cannot be reliably assessed from this dataset.

    Top open rates span dates from July 2025 through May 2026 with no clustering around specific days

    The data includes send dates but not days of the week, and high open rates appear across all months represented. Without day-of-week data and controlling for campaign type and list size, no send-timing pattern can be responsibly extracted.

    Data quality notes: 61 emails span a wide range of audience sizes (1 to 848 recipients), which severely skews percentage-based metrics—several '100% open rate' results come from single-recipient sends and carry no statistical weight. At least 5 subject lines appear across multiple sends to different segments or at different times (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' sent twice, 'You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality' sent three times), making it difficult to isolate subject line performance from list quality or timing effects. Two emails contain unrendered HubSpot personalization tokens in the subject line, indicating QA gaps. Insights should be weighted toward sends of 50+ recipients; small-list results are excluded from actionable benchmarks.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 68 30.9% 4.4%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 88 27.3% 1.1%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 90 24.4% 1.1%
    Final Call: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner at Boston’s Acclaimed TABLE 63 22.2% 3.2%
    One Week Away: Dinner at The Finch – Few Spots Left 60 21.7% 1.7%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing micro event emails share two consistent traits: they name a specific, recognizable venue or experience (Flour + Water, Juniper and Ivy, Golden Knights) and they use casual, conversational subject lines rather than formal 'exclusive' language. Emails sent to very small lists (2–30 recipients) dominate the top open rates, suggesting tight audience targeting amplifies performance, but even mid-size sends like the 68-person Boston dinner hit 30.9% when venue specificity and urgency were combined. The Nashville and Atlanta campaigns consistently underperformed, possibly due to audience fit rather than subject line quality. Future campaigns should lead with the specific venue name or experience and reserve 'exclusive' framing for urgency nudges rather than the initial invitation.

    Strong Signal

    Naming the specific venue or experience in the subject line is associated with higher open rates.

    Top 6 emails by open rate (30%+) all name a specific venue or branded experience

    Try this next: Always include the specific venue name or event name in the subject line rather than relying solely on 'exclusive dinner' framing.

    The Golden Knights experience campaign had the highest average click rate among multi-send campaigns.

    Three Golden Knights emails averaged 11.0% click rate (12.5%, 11.5%, 9.1%) versus ~3–4% for most dinner campaigns

    Try this next: When offering experiential (non-dinner) events, use them as anchor campaigns for high-value prospects, as they appear to drive stronger click-through intent.

    Very small send lists (under 15 recipients) inflate open rates and make performance comparisons unreliable.

    5 of the top 8 open rates come from sends of 30 or fewer recipients

    Try this next: Weight insights toward sends of 25+ recipients when evaluating subject line performance; use small sends as qualitative signals only.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, short-form subject lines (especially those using '@' shorthand) outperform formal invitation language.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (38.9%) and 'You're Invited to Dinner @ Juniper and Ivy' (40.0%) both outperformed longer formal equivalents
    Show reasoning

    The two '@'-style subjects averaged ~39% open rates versus ~20% for formally worded counterparts in similar campaigns. However, the sample of '@'-style subjects is small (3 emails, one with only 1 send), so this is directional rather than conclusive.

    Urgency-framing ('Last Call', 'Final Seats', 'Don't Miss') in follow-up emails can sustain solid open rates but rarely exceeds the initial send.

    'Last Call: Golden Knights' hit 30.8% and 'Final Seats: Boston' hit 30.9%, comparable to their initial sends
    Show reasoning

    Urgency follow-ups for the Golden Knights and Boston Healthcare events held open rates near or above 30%, suggesting they work for re-engagement. However, the 'Last Chance' Nashville follow-up dropped to 14.8%, indicating urgency language alone does not rescue a poor-performing campaign.

    The Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience campaign had 0% open rates across both sends, underperforming all other experience-based emails.

    0.0% open rate across both sends (5 and 9 recipients) versus 27–33% for Golden Knights initial sends
    Show reasoning

    Both Sphere Experience sends were in February 2026 and went to small lists (5 and 9), so the sample is tiny. However, the contrast with the Golden Knights campaign is stark. It is unclear whether the difference is due to audience fit, timing, or lower brand recognition of the Sphere/Wizard of Oz event.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear directional pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates span subjects from 7 words ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') to 17 words ('In Vegas? Join us for Medallion's Power Play...')

    Both very short and quite long subject lines appear across the top and bottom of the open rate rankings. No consistent length threshold separates high from low performers. Venue specificity and audience targeting appear to be stronger drivers than character count.

    Send timing (day of week or time of send) cannot be evaluated from this dataset.

    No send-time data available; dates range across multiple months with no time-of-day information

    The dataset includes send dates but not send times or days of the week in a way that maps cleanly to open rate variation. Campaign topics and target geographies differ across dates, making date-based attribution unreliable.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines were sent to multiple segments on different dates (e.g., 'In Vegas? Join us for Medallion's Power Play' appears twice on the same date with 3 and 11 recipients; 'A Seat at the Table: Aquavit' appears twice to ~88–90 recipients; 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' appears three times). This segmented sending means open rates across these sends should not be averaged without knowing whether they targeted distinct audiences. Audience sizes range from 1 to 90, creating high variance in rate reliability — emails sent to fewer than 15 recipients should be treated as anecdotal. No click-through destination data or conversion (RSVP) data is available, limiting the ability to assess full-funnel performance.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 108 43.5% 2.8%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 102 40.2% 3.9%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 109 34.9% 3.7%
    When a local hospital closes, are you ready? 51 33.3% 0.0%
    The hidden crisis behind hospital closures 51 19.6% 2.0%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing emails in this dataset combine specific financial framing (dollar amounts, revenue impact) with urgency signals like brackets and 'invited' or 'final chance' language, consistently outperforming vaguer operational themes. High open rates cluster around emails sent to smaller, likely more targeted segments, which inflates apparent performance for several top entries. The most reliable insight across larger sends (100+ recipients) is that report-style and data-driven subject lines outperform narrative or question-based ones. To improve, prioritize quantified pain points in subject lines and reserve bracket formatting for genuinely exclusive or time-limited sends.

    Strong Signal

    Repeated sends of the same subject line to different segments show meaningful open rate variance, suggesting audience segment quality matters more than subject line alone.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment' sent three times same day: 43.5% (n=108), 30.8% (n=39), 20.8% (n=24)

    Try this next: Prioritize segment quality and targeting precision over subject line optimization alone — audit how each segment is defined before attributing performance differences to copy.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines with bracketed content-type labels or urgency cues drive the highest open rates on larger sends.

    43.5% open rate for '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' at n=108
    Show reasoning

    The two largest sends (n=108 and n=102) with bracket-formatted or specific data framing both exceeded 40% open rates, while most non-bracketed, narrative subject lines on comparable send sizes fall below 35%. However, the bracketed 'Final chance for coffee' and 'You're invited' emails were sent to only 7-8 recipients, making it impossible to separate format from audience targeting effects.

    Emails referencing a specific financial cost or revenue impact in the subject line outperform those with only operational language.

    57.1% open rate for subject line referencing '$1M+' cost impact vs. ~27-35% average for operational-theme emails
    Show reasoning

    The highest open rate in the dataset belongs to the email explicitly citing a $1M+ cost, and the second-highest references 'time to revenue' with urgency. However, both were sent to very small lists (n=7 and n=8), so this finding is directional rather than conclusive at scale.

    Question-based or vague operational subject lines underperform compared to data-led or outcome-specific ones.

    Emails like 'Ready to see what operational resilience looks like in action?' averaged 26% open rate across two sends (n=36, n=28)
    Show reasoning

    Subject lines framed as soft invitations or vague readiness questions consistently land in the bottom half of the open rate distribution, while those citing specific data points or financial stakes perform better. The pattern is consistent across multiple sends but confounded by potential segment quality differences.

    The coffee-themed or casual subject line series underperformed relative to the broader dataset average.

    '☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue?' achieved only 14.3% open rate (n=7), while the follow-up 'Final chance for coffee' hit 50% (n=8)
    Show reasoning

    The initial coffee-themed send (with emoji) dramatically underperformed, while the urgency-framed follow-up to a similarly small list did much better. This suggests the casual framing without urgency or specificity does not resonate, though the tiny send sizes make this inconclusive.

    Hospital closure and healthcare access crisis themes generate consistent mid-range engagement across multiple sends.

    Four emails on hospital closure themes averaged ~30.5% open rate across sends totaling 198 recipients
    Show reasoning

    'When hospitals close,' 'When a local hospital closes,' and 'The hidden crisis behind hospital closures' all cluster between 19.6% and 34.9%, showing reliable but not exceptional engagement. This theme performs steadily but does not break out to the top tier without added urgency or financial specificity.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear relationship with open rate performance in this dataset.

    Top performer has 79 characters; bottom performers range from short to long

    Both short and long subject lines appear across the full range of open rates. Without controlling for audience size and segment quality, no length-based pattern is visible in this data.

    Send timing (day or month) does not produce a distinguishable pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates span April 2026, May 2026, January 2026, and July 2025 with no clustering

    High-performing emails are distributed across multiple months and days, and the dataset does not include send-time data. Audience size variation makes it impossible to isolate a timing signal.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is a significant caveat throughout: 10 of 28 emails were sent to fewer than 50 recipients, and the top two open rates come from sends of only 7 and 8 people — a single extra open shifts rates by 12-14 percentage points, making these statistically unreliable. Several subject lines were sent multiple times on the same date to different segments (the [Report] subject appears three times on 2026-01-21; 'When a local hospital closes' appears twice on 2025-07-17; and several other subjects have exact duplicates), indicating A/B or segment tests rather than independent sends — these should not be averaged together without knowing segment definitions. Click rate data is sparse (18 of 28 emails show 0.0% clicks), limiting any CTA-based analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing announcement emails in this dataset tend to be sent to very small audiences (2–5 recipients), which inflates open rates and makes direct comparisons to larger sends misleading. Among the more statistically meaningful sends, subject lines that reference specific, tangible outcomes or named features (e.g., 'Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility') outperform generic feature-launch phrasing. Click rates are nearly universally zero across the dataset, suggesting recipients open but rarely act — the one notable exception involves an enrollment workflow improvement. Future sends should prioritize action-oriented, benefit-first subject lines and test stronger in-email CTAs to convert opens into clicks.

    Strong Signal

    Subject lines containing '$43M raised' consistently underperformed across all three audience segments tested.

    Across 3 variants sent to 34, 42, and 2 recipients, open rates were 23.5%, 40.5%, and 0% respectively — averaging well below the dataset median.

    Try this next: Avoid leading with funding news in subject lines for this audience; instead frame announcements around the customer benefit or capability unlocked by the investment.

    Click rates are near-zero across the entire dataset regardless of open rate.

    22 of 25 emails had a 0.0% click rate; the highest click rate observed was 33.3% (n=3).

    Try this next: Audit the in-email CTAs across announcement emails — add one explicit, low-friction action (e.g., 'See it in your dashboard') to every send and A/B test CTA placement and copy.

    Emails sent to very small audiences (n≤5) dominate the top of the open-rate ranking, distorting apparent performance.

    9 of the top 11 open-rate results come from sends with 5 or fewer recipients.

    Try this next: Establish a minimum send size threshold (e.g., n≥15) before using open rate data to draw subject line conclusions, and track absolute open counts alongside rates.

    Moderate Signal

    Benefit-first or action-oriented subject lines correlate with higher open rates in larger sends.

    66.7% open rate for 'Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility' (n=3) vs. 23.5% for '$43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse' (n=34)
    Show reasoning

    Subjects that lead with a workflow benefit ('Submit... easier', 'Unlocking faster') tend to outperform funding-announcement or generic 'New feature' subjects in the mid-to-large send tiers. However, small audience sizes for the benefit-led emails limit confidence, and the funding emails had the largest audiences, introducing possible segment quality differences.

    'Coming soon' preview emails do not clearly outperform 'Now live' launch emails.

    'Coming soon: Provider profile redesign' opened at 75% (n=4) vs. 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' at 50% (n=2).
    Show reasoning

    There are matched pairs in the data for 'coming soon' vs. 'now live' on the same feature, but audience sizes are too small (2–4) to draw firm conclusions. The directional signal slightly favors anticipation-building previews, but this could easily be noise.

    Repeated subject lines sent to different segments show inconsistent open rates, suggesting audience quality varies by segment.

    'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' scored 100% (n=2) and 0% (n=1) on different send dates.
    Show reasoning

    The same subject line sent to different micro-segments on different dates produced wildly different results, reinforcing that audience composition and segment engagement level are confounding the open rate signal throughout this dataset.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short ('Generate API keys directly in Medallion', 9 words) and long ('New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding', 12 words) subjects.

    Both short and long subject lines appear at the top and bottom of the open rate distribution. The dominant factor appears to be audience size rather than subject length, making it impossible to isolate a length effect from this data.

    Send timing (day of week or month) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are spread across October, November, December, January, February, and March with no clustering.

    Top open rates occur across multiple months and no single time period stands out. Without send-time or day-of-week data, and given the audience-size confound, no timing recommendation is supported by the data.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is small (25 emails total) and audience sizes vary dramatically — from 1 to 42 recipients per send. The majority of 'top performers' by open rate were sent to audiences of 2–5 people, making those rates statistically unreliable (a single open moves the rate by 20–50 points). Several subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments on different dates (e.g., '$43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse' sent on both 2025-08-18 and 2025-08-20; 'New Analytic reports' variants sent across multiple dates), which fragments the signal and prevents clean A/B comparisons. Conclusions drawn from any individual email should be treated as directional only; the funding-announcement emails (n=34 and n=42) are the only sends large enough to support moderate statistical inference.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails are the personal 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' series, which consistently outperform the branded 'Medallion Product Update' format by a wide margin in open rates. However, the roundup emails were sent to very small audiences (3–8 recipients), making direct comparison with the product update emails (sent to 24–25 recipients) statistically unreliable. Notably, click rates across almost all emails are zero or near-zero, suggesting that while the roundup subject line format drives opens, neither format is effectively converting readers to action. The priority shift should be toward understanding why clicks are absent before over-investing in subject line optimization.

    Strong Signal

    Click rates are effectively zero across almost the entire campaign set.

    9 out of 11 emails have a 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA placement of all newsletter formats — introduce one clear, specific call-to-action per email and A/B test it against the current no-CTA or buried-CTA approach.

    Very small send lists (under 10 recipients) inflate apparent open rates and make performance metrics unreliable.

    Emails sent to 3–6 recipients show 50–66.7% open rates; emails sent to 24–25 show 25–44%

    Try this next: Grow the Roundup subscriber list to at least 20+ recipients before drawing optimization conclusions from its open rate data.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines achieve higher open rates than branded product update lines.

    66.7% avg open rate (Roundup) vs. 36.0% avg open rate (Product Update)
    Show reasoning

    Every Roundup email outperforms every Product Update email in open rate, suggesting the personal, possessive framing ('Your') and conversational tone resonate more. However, the Roundup emails were sent to 3–8 recipients while Product Update emails went to 24–25, so audience size and list composition likely differ meaningfully, limiting confidence.

    Shorter, personalized subject lines outperform longer branded ones in this dataset.

    'Your August Roundup ✨' (22 chars) at 66.7% vs. 'Medallion Product Update - February 2026' (40 chars) at 25.0%
    Show reasoning

    The Roundup subjects are shorter and more personal, while the Product Update subjects are longer and include the brand name and date. The pattern is directional but confounded by audience size differences — it is not possible to isolate subject length as the sole variable from this data alone.

    The ✨ emoji in subject lines appears consistently across all top-performing Roundup emails.

    All 6 Roundup emails use ✨; top 5 open rates (50–66.7%) all belong to Roundup emails
    Show reasoning

    Every Roundup subject line includes the ✨ emoji and no Product Update email does, so it is impossible to isolate the emoji's contribution from other differences in framing, audience, and send size. The pattern is consistent but fully confounded.

    No Signal

    No clear send-timing pattern emerges from this data.

    Emails sent across Tuesdays, Wednesdays, and Mondays with no consistent open-rate grouping by day

    The data does not include send time, and the send dates span different days of the week without a discernible pattern correlating to open rate performance. No timing conclusion is supportable here.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 11 emails with send volumes ranging from 1 to 25 recipients — all figures are statistically fragile. The Roundup emails (sent to 3–8 people) and the Product Update emails (sent to 24–25 people) appear to target different audience segments, making head-to-head comparisons unreliable. One email ('Your June Roundup ✨') was sent to only 1 recipient and recorded 0% opens, which is an effective non-data-point. No subject lines were repeated across sends. Any patterns identified should be treated as directional hypotheses requiring validation on larger lists.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 201 37.3% 9.0%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 176 34.7% 6.8%
    Your insights are needed! 214 26.2% 6.1%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 215 21.9% 6.5%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails in this dataset combined personalization, monetary incentives, and urgency — with the highest-volume performers showing that '$20' offers paired with personalized subject lines outperformed generic 'your insights are needed' framing. The 'last call' urgency email also outperformed the earlier generic version, suggesting deadline framing lifts opens. The main takeaway: lead with the incentive and personalization upfront, and use urgency-based follow-ups rather than vague calls to action. Note that the top open rate email (50%) was sent to only 4 recipients, making it statistically unreliable.

    Strong Signal

    Very small recipient lists (n=4) produce misleadingly high open rates that should not guide strategy.

    50.0% open rate on 4 recipients vs. 21.9%–37.3% on 176–215 recipients

    Try this next: Exclude emails with fewer than 50 recipients from benchmarking calculations to avoid distorted averages.

    Moderate Signal

    Monetary incentive ($20) in subject lines drives higher open and click rates than generic feedback requests.

    9.0% click rate for '$20 for your insights!' vs. 6.1% for 'Your insights are needed!'
    Show reasoning

    The email offering '$20' in the subject line (37.3% open, 9.0% click) outperformed the generic 'Your insights are needed' email (26.2% open, 6.1% click) sent just 6 days earlier to a similar audience size. However, the sequence effect cannot be ruled out — the incentive email may have benefited from being a follow-up. The pattern is directional but not conclusive.

    Urgency framing ('Last call') in the subject line outperformed both non-urgent and incentive-only subject lines at scale.

    34.7% open rate for 'Last call' vs. 26.2% for 'Your insights are needed!'
    Show reasoning

    The 'Last call' email sent on 10/07 achieved the second-highest open rate among emails with meaningful send volumes (176 recipients), beating the earlier generic version and the incentive email in opens, though not in clicks. This suggests urgency drives awareness but the incentive may be needed to push action. Only one urgency email exists in the dataset, limiting confidence.

    Personalization tokens (first name) in subject lines appear to lift opens, but the sample is confounded.

    37.3% open rate for the personalized subject line vs. 21.9%–26.2% for non-personalized alternatives
    Show reasoning

    The only email using '{{ contact.firstname }}' achieved the highest open rate among large sends. However, this email also included the '$20' incentive, so the personalization effect cannot be isolated from the incentive effect. The pattern is promising but not independently evidenced.

    Framing the survey as 'your chance to provide feedback' underperforms compared to leading with the reward.

    21.9% open rate for 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' vs. 37.3% for '$20 for your insights!'
    Show reasoning

    The earliest email buries the '$20' incentive after 'Your chance to provide feedback,' achieving the lowest open rate (21.9%) despite having the largest send volume (215). The later email that leads with '$20' achieved 37.3% opens. This suggests front-loading the incentive in the subject line is more effective, though send-sequence fatigue could also explain the gap.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines do not clearly outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Top open rates span subject lines from 4 words to 9 words

    The top-performing large-send email has a mid-length subject ('{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' — ~6 tokens), while the 'Last call' email is longer (~9 words) and outperforms the shorter 'Your insights are needed!' (~4 words). No consistent length advantage is visible across this small dataset.

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) cannot be evaluated from this data.

    Dates range from 2025-09-17 to 2026-06-29 with no time-of-day data

    Only send dates are available, not send times, and all emails were sent across a 10-month window making performance differences attributable to many factors beyond timing. No send-day pattern can be reliably extracted from 5 emails.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 5 emails with a wide range of audience sizes (4 to 215 recipients), which severely limits statistical reliability. The top open-rate email (50%) was sent to just 4 recipients and should not be used for benchmarking. The three largest sends (176–215 recipients) were all deployed within a ~3-week window in September–October 2025 as part of what appears to be a sequential survey campaign, meaning performance differences may reflect audience fatigue or sequencing effects rather than subject line quality alone. No subject lines were repeated across sends. One subject line contains an unrendered personalization token ('{{ contact.firstname }}'), which may have affected open rates positively (curiosity) or negatively (appearing broken) depending on the email client and audience.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (4 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ No eligible emails for this content type and persona combination.

    –
    No pipeline data for the selected filters.

    Nurture Opportunities

    Coverage reality

    1 active nurture — Branched 2026 Onboarding Nurture (20 emails) — covering onboarding contacts only. All other segments below have no active nurture coverage.

    Opportunity Cards

    Operations — Pre-MQL

    38,975 contacts

    No active nurture coverage

    Suggested angle

    Post-event transactional subject lines ('Thank you,' 'We missed you,' 'Watch on demand') achieve the highest open rates in this dataset.

    Top 5 open rates (57.9%–80%) all belong to post-event or on-demand access emails.

    Based on webinar performance for this persona

    Starting point: a 4–5 email sequence over 3–4 weeks. Validate with a small test send before committing to the full path.

    ⚠ Informed by one-off send performance, not validated nurture data.
